pages.title: Introduction
pages.html:
Introduction

The present Seminar is the continuation of the 1960 Seminar. We refer to the latter by symbols such as I 7 which means: Séminaire de Géométrie Algébrique, exposé I, 9.7. The numbers of the 1961 exposés will follow those of 1960. We refer to the Éléments de Géométrie Algébrique of Dieudonné-Grothendieck by symbols such as (EGA I 8.7.3).

The present exposé summarizes (with slight additions) the last exposés of 1960, which had not been written up.

As in 1961, we shall as a rule restrict ourselves to locally Noetherian preschemes, although this restriction is often inessential. In exposé VI we shall admit the theory of faithfully flat descent, summarized in Séminaire Bourbaki 190. Should the occasion arise, we shall give a more detailed account of it in a later exposé1, once the reader has had the opportunity to convince himself of the usefulness of this technique, for the theory of the fundamental group.

1 Prescheme with a finite group of operators, quotient prescheme

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(G\) a finite group operating on \(X\) by automorphisms, on the right to fix ideas. If \(X\) is affine with ring \(A\), then \(G\) operates by automorphisms on the left on \(A\).

For every prescheme \(Z\), \(G\) operates on the left on the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,Z)\), one may therefore consider the set

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(X,Z)^G \]

of morphisms invariant under \(G\). It depends functorially on \(Z\), one may ask whether this functor is “representable”, i.e. isomorphic to a functor \(Z\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\operatorname{Hom}(Y,Z)\). This means that one can find a prescheme \(Y\), and a morphism invariant under \(G\)

\[ p\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y \]

such that for every \(Z\), the corresponding map \(g\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} gp\)

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(Y,Z)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(X,Z)^G \]

is bijective. One then says that \((Y,p)\) is a quotient prescheme of \(X\) by \(G\) (it is determined up to unique isomorphism).

Proposition 1

Let \(A\) be a ring on which the finite group \(G\) operates on the left, \(B=A^G\) the subring of invariants of \(A\), \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}(B)\), \(p\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) the canonical morphism (obviously invariant under \(G\)). Then

  1. (i)

    \(A\) is integral over \(B\), i.e. \(p\) is an integral morphism.

  2. (ii)

    The morphism \(p\) is surjective, its fibres are the orbits of \(G\), the topology of \(Y\) is the quotient of that of \(X\).

  3. (iii)

    Let \(x\in X\), \(y=p(x)\), \(G_x\) the stabilizer of \(x\); then \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is a quasi-Galois algebraic extension of \(\mathit{k}(y)\) and the canonical map of \(G_x\) into the group \(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathit{k}(x)/\mathit{k}(y))\) of \(\mathit{k}(y)\)-automorphisms of \(\mathit{k}(x)\) is surjective.

  4. (iv)

    \((Y,p)\) is a quotient prescheme of \(X\) by \(G\).

The statements (i), (ii), (iii) are well known in commutative algebra1 and are stated only for the record, except the assertion on the topology, which comes from the following general fact, an easy consequence of the Cohen-Seidenberg theorem: an integral morphism is closed (i.e. transforms closed sets into closed sets). Let us note at once:

Corollary 2

Under the preceding conditions, the natural homomorphism

\[ \mathscr{O}_Y{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}} p_*(\mathscr{O}_X)^G \]

is an isomorphism.

This follows immediately from the formula

\[ (S^{-1}A)^G = S^{-1}(A^G) \]

valid for every multiplicatively stable subset \(S\) of \(B=A^G\) (a formula which localizes, and which is stated more generally for a base change \(A\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A'\) that is flat), applied to the case where \(S\) is generated by an element \(f\) of \(B\).

Assertion (ii) and cor. 2 easily imply (iv); more generally, we have the following:

Proposition 3

Let \(X\) be a prescheme with a group of finite automorphisms \(G\), \(p\colon{}X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{}Y\) an invariant affine morphism such that \(\mathscr{O}_Y\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} p_*(\mathscr{O}_X)^G\). Then the conclusions (i), (ii), (iii), (iv) of 1 are still valid.

Indeed, for (i), (ii), (iii), one may assume \(Y\), and hence \(X\), affine, and if \(B,A\) are their rings, the hypothesis implies \(B=A^G\); it suffices to apply 1. For (iv), one uses (ii) and \(\mathscr{O}_Y=p_*(\mathscr{O}_X)^G\).

Corollary 4

Under the conditions of 3, for every open set \(U\) of \(Y\), \(U\) is a quotient of \(X|U=p^{-1}(U)\) by \(G\).

Indeed, \(p^{-1}(U)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{}U\) induced by \(p\) satisfies the same hypotheses as \(p\).

If now \(X\) is a \(Z\)-prescheme and the operations of \(G\) are \(Z\)-automorphisms, then by (iv) \(Y\) is a \(Z\)-prescheme. This said:

Corollary 5

For \(X\) to be affine resp. separated over \(Z\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(Y\) be so. If \(X\) is of finite type over \(Z\), it is finite over \(Y\); if moreover \(Z\) is locally Noetherian, \(Y\) is of finite type over \(Z\).

Since \(X\) is affine and a fortiori separated over \(Y\), if \(Y\) is affine resp. separated over \(Z\), then \(X\) is so too. Conversely, suppose \(X\) affine over \(Z\); let us prove that \(Y\) is so: thanks to 4 one may assume \(Z\) affine, and one is reduced to proving that if \(X\) is affine, \(Y\) is so, which follows from the explicit determination of \(Y\) as \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)^G\) made in 1. Likewise, since \(p\colon{}X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{}Y\) is integral, hence universally closed, and surjective, it follows that if \(X\) is separated over \(Z\), then \(Y\) is so too (a lemma to be extracted!); indeed, in the diagram

\[ \xymatrix@C=1.5cm{{X \times_ZX}\ar[r]^-{p\times_Zp}&{Y\times_ZY}\\{X}\ar[u]^{\Delta_{X/Z}}\ar[r]^{p}&{Y}\ar[u]_{\Delta_{Y/Z}}} \]

the morphism \(X\times_ZX\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\times_ZY\) is closed, hence transforms the (closed) diagonal of \(X\times_ZX\) into a closed subset of \(Y\times_ZY\), which is moreover none other than the diagonal of the latter since \(p\) is surjective. — If \(X\) is of finite type over \(Z\), it is so a fortiori over \(Y\), hence it is finite over \(Y\) (since it is already integral over \(Y\)). Suppose moreover \(Z\) locally Noetherian; let us prove that \(Y\) is of finite type over \(Z\). Thanks to 4 one may assume \(Z\) affine. Since the topological space \(X\) is quasi-compact and \(p\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is surjective, \(Y\) is likewise quasi-compact, hence a finite union of affine open sets, and by 4 one is reduced to the case where \(Y\) is affine, hence \(X\) affine. But then the ring \(A\) of \(X\) is an algebra of finite type over the ring \(C\) of \(Z\), which is Noetherian, and it is known that \(B=A^G\) is then likewise an algebra of finite type over \(C\) (for \(A\) will be integral, hence finite over a subalgebra \(B'\) of \(B\) of finite type over \(C\), so that, since \(B'\) is Noetherian, \(B\) is likewise finite over \(B'\), hence of finite type over \(C\)).

Corollary 6

For \(X\) to be affine resp. a scheme, it is necessary and sufficient that \(Y\) be so.

Definition 7

Let \(X\) be a prescheme on which a finite group \(G\) operates on the right. One says that \(G\) operates in an admissible way if there exists a morphism \(p\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) having the properties of 3 (which implies that \(X/G\) exists and is isomorphic to \(Y\)).

Proposition 8

Let \(X\) be a prescheme on which the finite group \(G\) operates on the right. For \(G\) to operate in an admissible way, it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\) be a union of affine open sets invariant under \(G\), or equivalently that every orbit of \(G\) in \(X\) be contained in an affine open set.

This last condition is evidently implied by the first, and in turn it implies the first; for let \(T\) be a trajectory of \(G\), \(U\) an affine open containing it; the intersection of the transforms of \(U\) by the \(g\) in \(G\) is then an open set \(U'\) stable under \(G\), containing \(T\) and contained in the affine open \(U\). Since in \(U\) every finite subset has a fundamental system of affine open neighbourhoods, there exists an affine open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(T\) contained in \(U'\). Its transforms by the \(g\) in \(G\) are therefore affine and contained in \(U'\), which is separated, hence their intersection \(U''\) is an affine open set which is invariant under \(G\) and contains \(T\). — This being so, the condition considered in 8 is necessary, for one takes the inverse images \(X_i\) of affine opens \(Y_i\) covering \(Y\). It is sufficient, for one can then by 1 construct the quotients \(Y_i=X_i/G\); in each \(Y_i\) the image of \(X_i\cap X_j\) is an open set \(Y_{ij}\) identifying with \(X_{ij}/G\) by 4, in particular one deduces from this isomorphisms \(Y_{ij}\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} Y_{ji}\) allowing one to glue the \(Y_i\) together to construct \(Y\). Serre prefers to construct directly the quotient topological space \(Y\) of \(X\) by \(G\), to put on it the sheaf \(p_*(\mathscr{O}_X)^G\) and to verify that \(Y\) becomes a prescheme and that one is then under the conditions of 3.

Corollary 7

If \(G\) operating on \(X\) is admissible, the same holds for every subgroup \(H\) of \(G\) (hence \(X/H\) exists).

This can also be verified directly on the situation 3, by noting that one may always suppose \(X\) affine over some \(Z\) and the \(s\in G\) operating by \(Z\)-automorphisms (one takes for example \(Z=Y\)); indeed one has:

Corollary 8

Suppose \(X\) affine over \(Z\), and the operations of \(G\) \(Z\)-auto­mor­phisms. Then \(G\) operates on \(X\) in an admissible manner. If \(X\) is defined by a quasi-coherent sheaf \(\mathscr{A}\) of algebras, \(Y\) is defined by the sheaf \(\mathscr{A}^G\) of invariants of \(\mathscr{A}\) under \(G\).

Proposition 9

Suppose that \(G\) operates in an admissible manner on \(X\), and that \(X/G=Y\) is a prescheme over \(Z\). Consider a base-change morphism \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), set \(X'=X\times_Z Z', Y'=Y\times_Z Z'\), so that \(G\) still operates by transport of structure on \(X'\), the morphism \(p'\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y'\) being invariant. If \(Z'\) is flat over \(Z\), then \(p'\) still satisfies the hypotheses of 3, i.e. \(\mathscr{O}_Y'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} p_*'(\mathscr{O}_{X'})^G\) is an isomorphism (\(p'\) being in any case affine). Hence \(G\) operates in an admissible manner on \(X'\), and \((X/G)\times_Z Z'\thickapprox (X\times_Z Z')/G\).

One may evidently suppose \(Z=Y\); one is reduced to the case where moreover \(Y\) and \(Y'\) are affine. One must show that if \(B\) is the subring of invariants of \(G\) operating in \(A\), and if \(B'\) is an algebra over \(B\) flat over \(B\), then \(B'\) is the subalgebra of invariants of \(A'=A\otimes_B B'\). This is immediate, for the exact sequence .5cm

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} B \xrightarrow{i} A \xrightarrow{j} A^{(G)} \]

(where the last term denotes a power of \(A\), and where \(j(x)\) is the system of the \(s\cdot x-x\), \(s\in G\)) remains exact upon tensoring by \(B'\).

One should take care that the flatness hypothesis was essential for the validity of the result; in particular, if \(Y'\) is a closed sub-prescheme of \(Y\) (for example even a closed point of \(Y\)), \(X'\) its inverse image in \(X\), then \(Y'\) does not identify in general with \(X'/G\). We shall see that it is nevertheless so if \(X\) is étale over \(Y\).

To finish, let us give a formalism as convenient as it is trivial. Let \(Y\) be a prescheme. Since in the category of preschemes direct sums exist, one can for every set \(E\) consider the sum prescheme of a family \((Y_i)_{i\in E}\) of preschemes all identical to \(Y\); this prescheme will be denoted \(Y\times E\). It is characterized by the formula

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{Hom}(Y\times E,Z) = \operatorname{Hom}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(Y,Z)) \]

where the second \(\operatorname{Hom}\) evidently denotes the set of maps from the set \(E\) into the set \(\operatorname{Hom}(Y,Z)\). One has a canonical morphism

\[ Y\times E\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y \]

making \(Y\times E\) a prescheme over \(Y\). Since the fibre products commute with direct sums (in the category of preschemes) one will have, if \(Y\) is a prescheme over another one \(Z\), for a base change \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\):

\[ (Y\times E)\times_Z Z'=(Y\times_Z Z')\times E \]

(a formula especially useful if \(Z=Y\)). On the other hand, one concludes trivially from the definition

\[ (Y\times E)\times F=Y\times(E\times F)=(Y\times E)\times_Y(Y\times F) \]

(the last formula however resulting from the commutativity pointed out above).

For \(Y\) fixed, one may regard \(Y\times E\) as a functor in \(E\), with values in the preschemes over \(Y\), a functor which commutes with finite products by the preceding formula, (which allows one for example to associate to any ordinary group \(G\) a group scheme \(Y\times G\) over \(Y\), which will be finite over \(Y\) if \(Y\) is so, etc.). More generally, this functor is “left exact”, but we shall have no need to make use of it here. This functor also commutes trivially with direct sums, and it is also “right exact”, as one sees at once from the defining formula (*). In particular, if the finite group \(G\) operates on the right on the set \(E\), then it operates on the right on \(Y\times E\), and one has

\[ (Y\times E)/G = Y\times (E/G) \]

where in fact the quotient on the left-hand side satisfies the conditions of 3 (this is immediate).

\[ \tag{*} \operatorname{Hom}(Y\times E,Z) = \operatorname{Hom}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(Y,Z)) \]

\[ Y\times E\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y \]

making \(Y\times E\) a prescheme over \(Y\). Since the fibre products commute with direct sums (in the category of preschemes) one will have, if \(Y\) is a prescheme over another one \(Z\), for a base change \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\):
\[ (Y\times E)\times_Z Z'=(Y\times_Z Z')\times E \]
(a formula especially useful if \(Z=Y\)). On the other hand, one concludes trivially from the definition
\[ (Y\times E)\times F=Y\times(E\times F)=(Y\times E)\times_Y(Y\times F) \]
(the last formula however resulting from the commutativity pointed out above).

For \(Y\) fixed, one may regard \(Y\times E\) as a functor in \(E\), with values in the preschemes over \(Y\), a functor which commutes with finite products by the preceding formula, (which allows one for example to associate to any ordinary group \(G\) a group scheme \(Y\times G\) over \(Y\), which will be finite over \(Y\) if \(Y\) is so, etc.). More generally, this functor is “left exact”, but we shall have no need to make use of it here. This functor also commutes trivially with direct sums, and it is also “right exact”, as one sees at once from the defining formula (*). In particular, if the finite group \(G\) operates on the right on the set \(E\), then it operates on the right on \(Y\times E\), and one has
\[ (Y\times E)/G = Y\times (E/G) \]
where in fact the quotient on the left-hand side satisfies the conditions of 3 (this is immediate).

2, th.  2.

pages.title: Decomposition and inertia groups. Étale case
pages.html:
2 Decomposition and inertia groups. Étale case

Let \(G\) be a finite group operating on the right on the prescheme \(X\). If \(x\in X\), one calls decomposition group of \(x\) the stabilizer \(G_d(x)\) of \(x\). This group operates canonically (on the left) on the residue field \(\mathit{k}(x)\), and the set of elements of \(G_d(x)\) that operate trivially is called the inertia group of \(x\), denoted \(G_i(x)\).

Suppose that \(G\) operates on \(X\) in an admissible manner and that \(Y\) is a prescheme over a prescheme \(Z\). Let us fix a \(z\in Z\), and an algebraically closed extension \(\Omega\) of \(\mathit{k}(z)\) having a transcendence degree greater than that of the \(\mathit{k}(x)/\mathit{k}(z)\), where \(x\) is a point of \(X\) above \(z\). One may regard \(\operatorname{Spec}(\Omega)\) as a \(Z\)-scheme, and the points of \(X\) with values in \(\Omega\) correspond to the homomorphisms of \(\mathit{k}(z)\)-algebras \(\mathit{k}(x)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\Omega\), where \(x\) is a point of \(X\) above \(z\); since \(\Omega\) was taken large enough, every point \(x\) of \(X\) above \(z\) is the locality of a point of \(X\) with values in \(\Omega\). If \(X(\Omega)\) and \(Y(\Omega)\) denote respectively the set of points of \(X\) and \(Y\) with values in \(\Omega\), one has a natural map

\[ X(\Omega)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y(\Omega), \]

on the other hand, \(G\) operates on \(X(\Omega)\) and the preceding map is invariant under \(G\). This being posited, conclusions (ii) and (iii) of 3 may also be interpreted thus: the preceding map is surjective and identifies \(Y(\Omega)\) with the quotient \(X(\Omega)/G\). Moreover, if \(x\) is the locality of \(a\in X(\Omega)\), then the stabilizer of \(a\) in \(G\) is none other than the inertia group \(G_i(x)\). All this is moreover true without assuming \(\Omega\) “large enough”; this last hypothesis serves only to ensure that one can characterize the inertia group of any element of \(X\) above \(z\) as a “geometric” stabilizer. One thereby concludes at once, for example:

Proposition 1

Let us make a base extension \(Z'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Z\), whence \(X'=X\times_ZZ'\). Let \(x'\) be a point of \(X'\), \(x\) its image in \(X\), then one has \(G_i(x)=G_i(x')\).

It suffices, in the considerations above, to take for \(\Omega\) a large enough extension of \(\mathit{k}(z')\) (where \(z,z'\) are the images of \(x,x'\) in \(Z,Z'\)).

Proposition 2

Under the conditions of 3, suppose \(Y\) locally Noetherian, \(X\) finite over \(Y\). Let \(H\) be a subgroup of \(G\), consider \(X'=X/H\) (cf. 7), let \(x\in X\), \(x'\) its image in \(X'\) and \(y\) its image in \(Y\).

  1. (i)

    If \(H\supset G_d(x)\), then the homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_{x'}\) induces an isomorphism on the completions.

  2. (ii)

    If \(H\supset G_i(x)\), then the homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{x'}\) is étale i.e. \(X'\) is étale over \(Y\) at \(x'\).

Let \(Y_1=\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat{\mathscr{O}_y})\), let us make the base change \(Y_1\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\), one finds an \(X_1=X\times_Y Y_1\) finite over \(Y_1\), on which \(G\) operates, the quotient being \(Y_1\) by 9. Let \(y_1\) be the unique point of \(Y_1\) above \(y\), since \(\mathit{k}(y)=\mathit{k}(y_1)\), it follows that the fibre of \(X\) at \(y\) is isomorphic to that of \(X_1\) at \(y_1\), whence a unique point \(x_1\) of \(X_1\) above \(x\). Moreover by 9 one will have \(X_1/H=X'_1=X'\times_Y Y_1\), let \(x'_1\) be the image of \(x_1\) in \(X'_1\), it is above \(x'\), and one verifies easily (\(X'\) being of finite type over \(Y\)) that the homomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_{x'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_{x'_1}\) induces an isomorphism on the completions. Thus one is reduced to the case where \(Y\) is the spectrum of a complete local ring, say \(B\), hence \(X\) the spectrum of a finite ring \(A\) over \(B\), composed of a finite number of local rings \(A_x\) corresponding to the points \(x_i\) of \(X\) over \(Y\). If \(A_0\) corresponds to \(x=x_0\), then \(A\) is identified with the ring \(\operatorname{Hom}_{G_d}(G,A_0)\) of functions \(f\colon G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_0\) such that \(f(st)=sf(t)\) for \(s\in G_d\), the operations of \(G\) on these functions being defined by \((uf)(t)=f(tu)\). One thus sees that if \(H\) is any subgroup of \(G\), then \(A^H\) is the ring of functions \(f\colon G\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A_0\) such that

\[ f(stu)=sf(t)\qquad\text{for}\ s\in G_d,\ u\in H \]

hence it is a semi-local ring whose local components correspond to the double cosets \(G_daH\) in \(G\), to the double coset defined by \(a\in G\) corresponding (thanks to the map \(f\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} f(a)\)) the subring \(A_0^{H(a)}\) of \(A_0\), where \(H(a)=G_d\cap aHa^{-1}\). Moreover, the local component of \(A^H\) corresponding to the image \(x'\) of \(x\) is also the one corresponding to the double coset \(G_dH\) of the neutral element, its local component is therefore \(A_0^{G_d\cap H}\). If therefore \(G_d\subset H\), one finds \(A_0^{G_d}=A^G=B\), which proves (i). To prove (ii), one may, by passing to a suitable finite flat extension of \(A\), and using 1, reduce to the case where the residual extension \(\mathit{k}(x)/\mathit{k}(y)\) is trivial. But then \(G_i(x)=G_d(x)\), and one is reduced to the preceding case.

Corollary 3

Under the conditions of 2, suppose \(G_i(x)=(e)\), then X is étale over \(Y\) at \(x\). Hence if \(G_i(x)=(e)\) for every \(x \in X\), then \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is an étale morphism.

There is a partial converse:

Corollary 4

Suppose \(X\) connected and the group \(G\) faithful on \(X\). For \(p \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y=X/G\) to be étale, it is necessary and sufficient that the inertia groups of the points of \(X\) be reduced to the neutral element. If this is so, \(G\) is identified with the group of all \(Y\)-automorphisms of the \(Y\)-scheme \(X\).

Taking 3 into account, one may suppose \(X\) étale over \(Y\). But if an \(s \in G\) is in some \(G_i(x)\), it then follows from I 4 that \(s\) operates trivially on \(G\), hence is the unit element since \(G\) is faithful, which proves the first assertion. Let \(u\) be a \(Y\)-automorphism of \(X\), let \(x \in X\). By proposition 3, there exists an \(s \in G\) such that \(s(x)=u(x)\), and inducing the same residual homomorphism \(\mathit{k}(x)/\mathit{k}(x')\) as \(u\). By loc. cit. one therefore has \(s=u\), which completes the proof.

Remark 5

The hypothesis that \(G\) operates faithfully is evidently not superfluous in corollary 4. The same holds for the hypothesis that \(X\) is connected, as one sees for example by taking \(X=Y \times E\), \(E\) being a finite set, and \(G\) the group of permutations of \(E\): \(G\) operates with much inertia, nevertheless \((Y \times E)/G=Y \times (E/G)=Y\), and \(X\) is étale over \(Y\). Taking for \(G\) a group strictly smaller than the symmetric group of \(E\), but operating transitively on \(E\), one sees that there will also be \(Y\)-automorphisms of \(X\) not arising from \(G\).

The typical example of a group \(G\) operating without inertia is that of \(Y \times G\), on which one makes \(G\) operate thanks to its operations on the factor \(G\) by right translations: a \(Y\)-prescheme \(X\) with right operator group \(G\) is said to be trivial if it is isomorphic to \(Y \times G\).

To make the connection between preschemes with finite operator groups and the notion of a principal bundle in a category (a connection which we shall moreover not need for the sequel of the seminar, but important in other contexts) the following considerations are useful. We fix a base prescheme \(Y\), and we place ourselves in the category of \(Y\)-preschemes. If \(G\) is a finite group, we shall set for brevity \(G_Y=Y \times G\), it is therefore a finite group scheme over \(Y\) (cf. nº 1), and if \(X\) is a \(Y\)-prescheme, one has

\[ X \times_Y G_Y = X \times G \]

(same reference). The datum of a \(Y\)-morphism \(X \times_Y G_Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is therefore equivalent to the datum of a \(Y\)-morphism \(X \times G \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), i.e. to the datum, for every \(g \in G\), of a \(Y\)-morphism \(T_g \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). One checks immediately that, in order for the datum of the \(T_g\) to define on \(X\) a structure of prescheme with right group of operators \(G\), (i.e. \(T_{gg'}=T_{g'}T_g\), \(T_e=\mathrm{id}_x\)) it is necessary and sufficient that the corresponding \(Y\)-morphism \(X \times_Y G_Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) define on \(X\) a structure of \(Y\)-prescheme with \(Y\)-group scheme of operators, (in the general sense of objects with \(\mathscr{C}\)-group of operators in a category \(\mathscr{C}\)). Suppose this is the case. Recall that \(X\) is said to be formally principal homogeneous under \(G_Y\)1 if the canonical morphism

\[ X \times_Y G_Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \]

whose components are respectively \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and the multiplication morphism \(\pi \colon X \times_Y G_Y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), is an isomorphism. In this instance, identifying the first member with \(X \times G\), the morphism under consideration is the one which, to every \(g \in G\), associates the morphism

\[ (\mathrm{id}_X,T_g)=(\mathrm{id}_X \times_Y T_g)\Delta_{X/Y} \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \]

and consequently, to say that \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G_Y\) also means that \(X \times_Y X\) is isomorphic to the direct sum of the transforms of the diagonal by the elements \((e,g)\) of \(G \times G\) (operating on \(X \times_Y X\) in the obvious way) where \(e\) denotes the unit element of \(G\). If one does not wish to distinguish left and right and to give a formula that remains applicable to the product of more than two factors identical to \(X\), one can formulate the condition by saying that the canonical morphism

\[ X \times_G (G \times G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \]

obtained by attaching to the pair \((g,g')\) the morphism

\[ (T_g,T_{g'})=(T_g \times_Y T_{g'})\Delta_{X/Y} \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \]

and by letting \(G\) operate on the left on \(G \times G\) through the diagonal homomorphism;

\[ s(g,g')=(sg,sg'), \]

is an isomorphism.

The notion of principal homogeneous space is deduced from that of the formally principal homogeneous space by adding a supplementary axiom, ensuring that the “quotient” of \(X\) by \(G_Y\) exists and is precisely the right unit object of the category, here \(Y\). This axiom may vary according to the context, and is most often made explicit most conveniently (in the yoga of “descent”) by requiring that the object with operators become “trivial”, i.e. isomorphic to the product \(X \times_Y G_Y\) (in this instance \(X \times G\)) by a suitable base change, of a specified type (in such a way, in practice, as to permit the technique of descent; cf. Grothendieck, Technique de descente et théorèmes d'existence en Géométrie Algébrique, Sém. Bourbaki 190, pages 26 to 28)2. In this order of ideas, let us point out here the characterization of the principal homogeneous bundles with group \(G\) (in the sense of loc. cit. ):

Proposition 6

Let \(Y\) be a locally Noetherian prescheme, \(X\) a \(Y\)-prescheme with finite group \(G\) of operators operating on the right. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(X\) is finite over \(Y\), \(Y=X/G\), the inertia groups of the points of \(X\) are reduced to the unit.

  2. (ii)

    There exists a faithfully flat and quasi-compact base change \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) such that \(X_1=X \times_Y Y_1\) is a \(Y_1\)-prescheme with trivial operators, i.e. isomorphic to \(Y_1 \times G\).

  3. (ii bis)

    As in (ii), but with \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) being finite, étale, surjective.

  4. (iii)

    \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G_Y\), and faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y\).

Proof (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis) We take \(Y_1=X\), noting that \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is indeed finite, étale by 3 and surjective. Let us show that \(X_1\) is then trivial over \(Y_1\), which will follow from the

Corollary 7

If (i) is satisfied and if \(X\) admits a section over \(Y\), then \(X\) is a space with trivial operators.

Indeed, this section allows one to define a \(G\)-morphism \(X \times G \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), surjective since \(G\) is transitive on the fibres of \(X\), injective since \(G\) operates without inertia; finally, it is a local isomorphism by virtue of I 3 since \(X\) is étale over \(Y\). Hence it is an isomorphism.

(ii bis) trivially implies (ii), which implies (i) because the ingredients of (i) are “invariant” under faithfully flat quasi-compact extension of the base (cf. the Séminaire Bourbaki cited above for “finite”; for the inertia groups, one applies 1, and for \(Y=X/G\), a converse of 9 in the case of a faithfully flat base change, which we had forgotten to make explicit).

We have proved (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) in passing while proving (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis). Finally (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), because the first hypothesis in (iii) means precisely that \(X\) becomes trivial upon making the base change \(Y_1=X\); whence (ii) since \(X\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y\).

Definition 8

A \(Y\)-prescheme \(X\) with right group of operators \(G\) satisfying the equivalent conditions of 6 is called a principal covering of \(Y\), with Galois group \(G\).

In this instance, identifying the first member with \(X \times G\), the morphism under consideration is the one which, to every \(g \in G\), associates the morphism
\[ (\mathrm{id}_X,T_g)=(\mathrm{id}_X \times_Y T_g)\Delta_{X/Y} \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \]

and consequently, to say that \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G_Y\) also means that \(X \times_Y X\) is isomorphic to the direct sum of the transforms of the diagonal by the elements \((e,g)\) of \(G \times G\) (operating on \(X \times_Y X\) in the obvious way) where \(e\) denotes the unit element of \(G\). If one does not wish to distinguish left and right and to give a formula that remains applicable to the product of more than two factors identical to \(X\), one can formulate the condition by saying that the canonical morphism
\[ X \times_G (G \times G) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \]
obtained by attaching to the pair \((g,g')\) the morphism
\[ (T_g,T_{g'})=(T_g \times_Y T_{g'})\Delta_{X/Y} \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \times_Y X \]
and by letting \(G\) operate on the left on \(G \times G\) through the diagonal homomorphism;
\[ s(g,g')=(sg,sg'), \]
is an isomorphism.

The notion of principal homogeneous space is deduced from that of the formally principal homogeneous space by adding a supplementary axiom, ensuring that the “quotient” of \(X\) by \(G_Y\) exists and is precisely the right unit object of the category, here \(Y\). This axiom may vary according to the context, and is most often made explicit most conveniently (in the yoga of “descent”) by requiring that the object with operators become “trivial”, i.e. isomorphic to the product \(X \times_Y G_Y\) (in this instance \(X \times G\)) by a suitable base change, of a specified type (in such a way, in practice, as to permit the technique of descent; cf. Grothendieck, Technique de descente et théorèmes d'existence en Géométrie Algébrique, Sém. Bourbaki 190, pages 26 to 28)2. In this order of ideas, let us point out here the characterization of the principal homogeneous bundles with group \(G\) (in the sense of loc. cit. ):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6

Let \(Y\) be a locally Noetherian prescheme, \(X\) a \(Y\)-prescheme with finite group \(G\) of operators operating on the right. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(X\) is finite over \(Y\), \(Y=X/G\), the inertia groups of the points of \(X\) are reduced to the unit.

  2. (ii)

    There exists a faithfully flat and quasi-compact base change \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) such that \(X_1=X \times_Y Y_1\) is a \(Y_1\)-prescheme with trivial operators, i.e. isomorphic to \(Y_1 \times G\).

  3. (ii bis)

    As in (ii), but with \(Y_1 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) being finite, étale, surjective.

  4. (iii)

    \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G_Y\), and faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y\).

Proof (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis) We take \(Y_1=X\), noting that \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is indeed finite, étale by 3 and surjective. Let us show that \(X_1\) is then trivial over \(Y_1\), which will follow from the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

If (i) is satisfied and if \(X\) admits a section over \(Y\), then \(X\) is a space with trivial operators.

Indeed, this section allows one to define a \(G\)-morphism \(X \times G \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), surjective since \(G\) is transitive on the fibres of \(X\), injective since \(G\) operates without inertia; finally, it is a local isomorphism by virtue of I 3 since \(X\) is étale over \(Y\). Hence it is an isomorphism.

(ii bis) trivially implies (ii), which implies (i) because the ingredients of (i) are “invariant” under faithfully flat quasi-compact extension of the base (cf. the Séminaire Bourbaki cited above for “finite”; for the inertia groups, one applies 1, and for \(Y=X/G\), a converse of 9 in the case of a faithfully flat base change, which we had forgotten to make explicit).

We have proved (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) in passing while proving (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii bis). Finally (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), because the first hypothesis in (iii) means precisely that \(X\) becomes trivial upon making the base change \(Y_1=X\); whence (ii) since \(X\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(Y\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8

A \(Y\)-prescheme \(X\) with right group of operators \(G\) satisfying the equivalent conditions of 6 is called a principal covering of \(Y\), with Galois group \(G\).

footnotes.html:
one rather says nowadays: \(X\) is a
pseudo-torsor under \(G_Y\).
Cf. Exp. VIII for the theory of flat descent.

pages.title: Automorphisms and morphisms of étale coverings
pages.html:
3 Automorphisms and morphisms of étale coverings

Proposition 1

Let \(X\) be étale, separated, of finite type over \(Y\) locally Noetherian, and let \(G\) be a finite group operating on \(X\) by \(Y\)-automorphisms. Then \(G\) operates in an admissible way and the quotient prescheme \(X/G\) is étale over \(Y\).

One does not assume \(X\) finite over \(Y\); nevertheless \(X\) is quasi-projective over \(Y\), whence the existence of \(X/G\) thanks to 8. Let us first prove

Corollary 2

The morphism \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X/G\) is étale.

One may evidently assume \(G\) transitive on the set of connected components of \(X\), then, by considering the stabilizer of a connected component, that \(X\) is even connected. Finally, one may assume that \(G\) operates faithfully. But then one sees as in 4 that \(G\) operates without inertia, hence by 3 it follows that \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X/G\) is étale. One concludes thanks to the

Lemma 3 (afterthought to Exposé I)

Let \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be morphisms of finite type, \(x\) a point of \(X\), \(x'\) and \(y\) its images. Assume \(Y\) locally Noetherian. If two of the morphisms considered are étale at the marked points, so is the third.

It only remains to look at the case where \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) and \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) are étale at \(x\) and to prove that \(X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) is so at \(x'\) (which is the case we need for 1). Making a suitable flat extension of the base \(Y\), one is reduced to the case where the residue extension \(\mathit{k}(x)/\mathit{k}(y)\) is trivial. Consider the homomorphisms \(\mathscr{O}_y \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_{x'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{O}_x\) and the homomorphisms deduced by passage to the completions; the hypothesis means that \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}_y} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{\mathscr{O}_x}\) and \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}_{x'}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{\mathscr{O}_x}\) are isomorphisms, whence at once that \(\widehat{\mathscr{O}_y} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{\mathscr{O}_{x'}}\) is one, which proves the lemma.

Corollary 4

If \(X\) is finite and étale over \(Y\), then \(X/G\) is finite and étale over \(Y\).

Proposition 5

Let \(X\), \(X'\) be two étale coverings of \(Y\). Then every \(Y\)-morphism \(f \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) factors as the composite of a surjective étale morphism \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X''\) and of the canonical immersion \(X'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) of a part \(X''\) of \(X'\) that is both open and closed.

One knows (I 8) that \(f\) is étale, hence an open morphism; on the other hand, \(X\) being finite over \(Y\), \(f\) is closed, hence \(f(X)=X''\) is a part of \(X'\) both open and closed. We are done (N.B. it would have sufficed that \(X'\), instead of an étale covering, be unramified over \(Y\)).

Corollary 6

With the preceding notations, \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is a strict epimorphism in the category of preschemes, and \(X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X''\) is a monomorphism (and even a strict monomorphism) in the category of preschemes.

The first assertion means by definition that the sequence of morphisms

\[ \xymatrix@C=.7cm{{X \times_{X''} X}\ar@<2pt>[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_1}\ar@<-2pt>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2}&{X}\ar[r]&{X''}} \]

is exact, and this results from the fact that \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X''\) is finite and faithfully flat, as one sees easily (cf. Grothendieck, loc. cit. ). The dual assertion for \(X'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is even more trivial.

Corollary 6 will be useful to us for the theory of the fundamental group in the next exposé; it is possible (for those who do not like the notion of strict epimorphism) to replace corollary 6 by whatever variant the reader will arrange to his personal taste. Let us merely take the occasion to point out that a factorization \(f=f'f''\), with \(f''\) a strict epimorphism and \(f'\) a monomorphism, is necessarily unique up to unique isomorphism (in any category); nevertheless, there may exist at the same time a factorization \(f=f_1f_2\) having the dual properties: \(f_2\) is an epimorphism, \(f_1\) a strict monomorphism, (likewise unique up to unique isomorphism), which is not isomorphic to the preceding one: it suffices to take, for example, the category of topological vector spaces (separated, if one insists), and for \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) a morphism such that \(u(X)\) is not closed.

Proposition 7

Let \(Y\) be a connected locally Noetherian prescheme, \(y\) a point of \(Y\), \(\mathit{\Omega}\) an algebraically closed extension of \(\mathit{k}(y)\). For every \(X\) over \(Y\), one denotes by \(X(\Omega)\) the set of points of \(X\) with values in \(\Omega\). Let \(X\), \(X'\) be étale coverings of \(Y\) and \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) a \(Y\)-morphism such that the corresponding map \(X(\Omega) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'(\Omega)\) is an isomorphism. Then \(u\) is an isomorphism.

One is immediately reduced to the case where \(X'\) is connected. Since \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is finite and étale, one knows that the geometric number of points in a fibre of \(X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) is constant, and equal to 1 if and only if the morphism considered is an isomorphism. Now this number is also the number of elements in a fibre of \(X(\Omega) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'(\Omega)\), whence the conclusion. .5cm
