pages.title: Axiomatic conditions for a Galois theory
pages.html:
4 Axiomatic conditions for a Galois theory

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, \(F\) a covariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of finite sets. Suppose the following conditions satisfied:

  1. (G 1)

    \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object1 and the fibred product of two objects over a third in \(\mathscr{C}\) exists (this axiom may also be stated by saying that in \(\mathscr{C}\) finite inverse limits exist).

  2. (G 2)

    Finite sums in \(\mathscr{C}\) exist (hence also an initial object \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), playing the role of the empty set), as well as the quotient of an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) by a finite group of automorphisms.

  3. (G 3)

    Let \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism in \(\mathscr{C}\), then \(u\) factors as a product \(X\xrightarrow{u'} Y'\xrightarrow{u''} Y\), with \(u'\) a strict epimorphism and \(u''\) a monomorphism, which is an isomorphism onto a direct summand of \(Y\).

  4. (G 4)

    The functor \(F\) is left exact (i.e. transforms a right unit into a right unit, and commutes with fibred products).

  5. (G 5)

    \(F\) commutes with finite direct sums, transforms strict epimorphisms into epi­mor­phisms, and commutes with passage to the quotient by a finite group of automorphisms.

  6. (G 6)

    Let \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism in \(\mathscr{C}\) such that \(F(u)\) is an isomorphism, then \(u\) is an isomorphism.

Our object is to construct a topological group \(\pi\), inverse limit of finite groups, and an equivalence of the category \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) of finite sets on which \(\pi\) operates continuously (i.e. in such a way that the stabilizer of a point is an open subgroup, or equivalently that there exists a discrete quotient group which already operates on the set considered), the equivalence constructed transforming the given functor \(F\) into the obvious inclusion functor of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) into the category of finite sets. We note at once that the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) constructed by means of a topological group \(\pi\), and the preceding inclusion functor, do indeed satisfy conditions (G 1) to (G 6).

We proceed in several steps.

  1. a)

    Let \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) in \(\mathscr{C}\). In order that \(u\) be a monomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(F(u)\) be so. (Uses (G 1), (G 4), (G 6)).

    Indeed, to say that \(u\) is a monomorphism means that the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1 \colon X \times_Y X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is an isomorphism.

  2. b)

    Every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) is Artinian.

    Indeed, if \(X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) are monomorphisms such that \(F(X')\) and \(F(X'')\) have the same image in \(F(X)\), then by a) \(F(X') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(X'')\) is an isomorphism, hence \(X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X''\) is an isomorphism by (G 6).

  3. c)

    The functor \(F\) is strictly pro-representable. (cf. Grothendieck, Technique de descente et théorèmes d'existence en Géométrie Algébrique, II, Séminaire Bourbaki 195, février 1960).

    Indeed, according to loc. cit. prop. 1, this follows from b) and (G 4). We can therefore find an inverse system over the filtered ordered set \(I\):

    \[ P=(P_i)_{i \in I} \]

    in \(\mathscr{C}\), regarded as a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), and a functorial isomorphism

    \[ \tag{*} F(X)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Pro}(\mathscr{C})}(P,X)=\varinjlim_{i} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}}(P_i,X) \]

    This isomorphism is realized by an element

    \[ \varphi \in \varprojlim_{i} F(P_i)=F(P) \]

    We may moreover assume that the transition homomorphisms \(\varphi_{ji} \colon P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_j\) (\(i \geqslant j\)) are epimorphisms, and that every epimorphism \(P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P'\) is equivalent to an epimorphism \(P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_j\) for a suitable \(j \leqslant i\) (which determines the inverse system \(P\) in an essentially unique way).

    An object \(X \in \mathscr{C}\) is said to be connected if it is not isomorphic to the sum of two objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) not isomorphic to the initial object \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

  4. d)

    The \(P_i\) are connected and not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

    If \(X\) is a left unit, we have \(F(X)=\emptyset\) by (G 5) applied to the direct sum of an empty family, and conversely by (G 6). Hence if \(X'\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) which is not a left unit, i.e. such that \(F(X') \neq \emptyset\), there exists no morphism from \(X'\) to \(X\). Hence if some \(P_i\) is a left unit, then \(i\) is a greatest element of the filtered ordered index set \(I\), and formula (*) would mean \(F(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,X)={}\) a set reduced to a single element for every \(X\), which is absurd since \(F(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}})=\emptyset\). Hence the \(P_i\) are not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

    Suppose we have \(P_i=A \amalg B\), whence by (G 5) \(F(P_i)=F(A) \amalg F(B)\); in particular the element \(a_i\) of \(F(P_i)\), corresponding by (*) to the identity homomorphism \(P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\), lies in \(F(A) \amalg F(B)\), for instance in \(F(A)\). This means that there exists a \(j \geqslant i\) such that \(\varphi_{ij} \colon P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) factors through \(P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i=A \amalg B\), where the second arrow is the canonical morphism. Hence \(F(P_j) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P_i)\) factors through \(F(P_j) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P_i)=F(A) \amalg F(B)\), and since \(F(P_j) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P_i)\) is surjective by (G 5), it follows that \(F(B)=\emptyset\), hence \(B\) is isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

  5. e)

    Every morphism \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) in \(\mathscr{C}\), with \(X\) not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) and \(Y\) connected, is a strict epimorphism. Every endomorphism of a connected object is an automorphism.

    Consider the factorization (G 3) of \(u\); since \(X \neq \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), it follows from (G 6) that \(F(X) \neq \emptyset\), hence \(F(Y') \neq \emptyset\), hence \(Y' \neq \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), so, \(Y\) being connected, \(Y'\) is identified with \(Y\), hence \(u\) is a strict epimorphism. Suppose that \(u\) is an endomorphism of the connected object \(X\); let us prove that it is an automorphism. Indeed, we may assume \(X\) not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), so \(u\) is a strict epimorphism by what precedes, hence \(F(u)\) is an epimorphism by (G 5), and since \(F(X)\) is a finite set, \(F(u)\) is bijective. Hence \(u\) is an automorphism by (G 6).

    In particular, every endomorphism of a \(P_i\) is an automorphism.

  6. f)

    The following conditions on a \(P_i\) are equivalent: (i) The natural injective map \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i) \simeq F(P_i)\) is also surjective, i.e. for every \(u \colon P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) there exists a \(v \colon P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) such that \(u=v\varphi_i\) (where \(\varphi_i\) is the canonical homomorphism \(P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\)). (ii) The group \(\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\) operates transitively on \(F(P_i)\). (iii) The group \(\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\) operates simply transitively on \(F(P_i)\).

    Indeed, identifying \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i)\) with \(F(P_i)\), the map considered in (i) is none other than \(v \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F(v)(\varphi_i)\). The equivalence of the three conditions then comes from the fact that \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i)=\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\) and that the preceding map is already injective.

    A \(P_i\) satisfying the equivalent conditions (i), (ii), (iii) of f) is called Galois.

  7. g)

    For every \(X\) in \(\mathscr{C}\), there exists a \(P_i\) Galois such that every \(u \in \operatorname{Hom}(P,X)\) factors through \(P \xrightarrow{\varphi_i} P_i \longrightarrow X\).

    Let \(J=\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)=F(X)\); this is a finite set, so there exists a \(P_j\) such that every \(u \colon P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) factors through \(P\xrightarrow{j}P_j\longrightarrow X\), or equivalently such that the natural morphism

    \[ P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J \qquad (J=\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) \]

    factors through

    \[ P \xrightarrow{\varphi_j} P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J \]

    By virtue of (G 3), the morphism \(P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J\) factors as the product of a monomorphism by a strict epimorphism, which one may take to be of the form \(\varphi_{ij} \colon P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\). We are thus reduced to proving that \(P_i\) is Galois. Let \(k\) be an index \(\geqslant j\) such that every morphism \(P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) factors through \(P \xrightarrow{\varphi_k} P_k \longrightarrow P_i\). Note that the natural morphism \(P_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J\) again factors as the composite

    \[ P_k \xrightarrow{\varphi_{ik}} P_i \xrightarrow{U} X^J \]

    where the first arrow is a strict epimorphism by e), and the second a monomorphism. We wish to prove that for a given morphism \(\psi \colon P_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\), there exists an endomorphism \(v\) of \(P_i\) such that \(\psi = v \varphi_{ik}\). But for every \(u \in \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,X)\), consider \(u \psi \in \operatorname{Hom}(P_k,X)\); it is therefore of the form \(u'\varphi_{ik}\) with a well-determined \(u' \in \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,X)\). The map \(u \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} u'\) from \(J\) to \(J\) thus defined by \(\psi\) is moreover injective, since \(\psi\) is an epimorphism by virtue of e); it is therefore bijective since the set \(J\) is finite. The bijective map \(u \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} u'\) from \(J\) to \(J\) thus defines an isomorphism \(\alpha \colon X^J \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X^J\) making the diagram commutative

    \[ \xymatrix{{P_k}\ar[r]^-{\varphi_{ik}}\ar@{=}[d]&{P_i}\ar[r]^-U&{X^J}\ar[d]^-{\alpha}_-{\simeq}\\{P_k}\ar[r]^{\psi}&{P_i}\ar[r]^-U&{X^J}} \]

    By the uniqueness properties of the factorization of a morphism as the product of a monomorphism by a strict epimorphism, it follows (since \(\psi\) too is a strict epimorphism by e)) that one can find a morphism \(v \colon P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) which keeps the diagram commutative, q.e.d.

    We conclude in particular that the Galois \(P_i\) form a cofinal system in the system of the \((P_j)\). We shall therefore have, since for a Galois object \(P_i\) we have

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i)=\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i)=\operatorname{Aut}(P_i), \]

    by passage to the limit:

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}(P,P) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Aut}(P_i) \]

    where .5cm the inverse limit is taken over the Galois \(P_i\). Moreover, by means of the identification \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i)=F(P_i)\), and taking into account that \(F\) transforms epimorphisms into epimorphisms, we see that the transition homomorphisms in the preceding inverse system are surjective. From all this we conclude:

  8. h)

    We have

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}(P,P) = \operatorname{Aut}(P) = \varprojlim_{i} F(P_i) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Aut}(P_i), \]

    where the inverse limit is taken over the \(P_i\) Galois.

    In particular, \(\operatorname{Aut}(P)\) appears as the inverse limit of an inverse system of finite groups (the transition homomorphisms being surjective); we shall endow it with the inverse limit topology of the discrete topologies. We shall denote by \(\pi\) and shall call the fundamental group (of \(\mathscr{C}\) equipped with \(F\)) the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(P)\). This group therefore operates on the right on \(P\); it is the inverse limit of finite groups \(\pi_i\) operating on the right on the Galois \(P_i\), \(\pi_i\) being the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\).

    Taking into account the functorial isomorphism

    \[ F(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \]

    and the definition of \(\pi\), we thus see that \(\pi\) operates on the left on \(F(X)\), and moreover continuously by g) (for, with the notation of g), it is in fact \(\pi_i\) that operates on \(F(X)\)). It is trivial that for every morphism \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) in \(\mathscr{C}\), the morphism \(F(u) \colon F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(Y)\) is compatible with the operations of \(\pi\). We may therefore henceforth regard \(F\) as a covariant functor

    \[ F \colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}(\pi) \]

    where \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) is the category of finite sets on which \(\pi\) operates continuously on the left.

    We shall now define a functor in the opposite direction:

    \[ G \colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \mathscr{C}(\pi) \]

    by the formula

    \[ G(E)=P \times_{\pi} E, \]

    where \(P \times_{\pi} E\) is defined as the solution of the universal problem summarized by

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}}(P \times_{\pi} E,X) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) \]

    (where in the second member \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)=F(X)\) is regarded as a set on which \(\pi\) operates on the left). We must prove the existence of the object \(P \times_{\pi} E\).

  9. i)

    Let \(Q\) be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) on which a finite group \(G\) operates on the right, and \(E\) a finite set on which \(G\) operates on the left. Then \(G \times_G E\) exists, and the canonical map

    \[ F(Q) \times_G E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(Q \times_G E) \]

    is an isomorphism.

    Since finite direct sums exist in \(\mathscr{C}\) by (G 2), and \(F\) commutes with them by (G 5), we are immediately reduced to the case where \(G\) operates transitively on \(E\); for if the \(E_j\) are the orbits of \(G\) in \(E\), we shall have

    \[ Q \times_G E = \underset {j}{\amalg}\ Q \times_G E_j. \]

    Let then \(a \in E\), let \(H\) be its stabilizer; one sees at once from the definition that \(Q \times_G E\) is identified with \(Q/H\). Whence the existence, thanks to (G 2), and the commutation property for \(F\), thanks to (G 5).

  10. j)

    Let \(E\) be an object of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), and let \(P_i\) be Galois such that \(\pi_i\) already operates on \(E\). Then \(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E\) exists and we have a canonical isomorphism

    \[ E \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E) \]

    If \(j \geqslant i\) is such that \(P_j\) is Galois, then the canonical homomorphism \(P_j \times_{\pi_j} E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i \times_{\pi_i} E\) is an isomorphism.

    The first assertion is a special case of i), taking into account that \(\pi_i\) operates simply transitively on \(F(P_i)\), which is equipped with a marked point \(\varphi_i\), whence an isomorphism \(F(P_i) \times_{\pi_i} E \simeq E\). For the second assertion one uses for example (G 6).

    For every \(j\), let \(\mathscr{C}_j\) be the full subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}\) formed by the \(X\) such that \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_j,X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \simeq F(X)\) is bijective. We know by g) that \(\mathscr{C}\) is the filtered union of the \(\mathscr{C}_j\). We therefore have for \(X \in \mathscr{C}_j\):

    \begin{align*} \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) & \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P_j,X)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi_j}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P_j,X))\\ & \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(P_j \times_{\pi_j} E,X) \end{align*}

    and taking into account the last assertion in j), we find an isomorphism functorial in the object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}_j\):

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E,X) \]

    Since this is true for every \(j\) and these functorial isomorphisms, for varying \(j\), induce one another, we conclude:

  11. k)

    Under the conditions of j), the morphism composed of the canonical morphisms

    \[ E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i \times_{\pi_i} E) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i \times_{\pi_i} E) \]

    makes of \(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E\) a solution of the universal problem defining \(P \times_{\pi} E\), i.e. the latter exists and we have an isomorphism

    \[ P \times_{\pi} E \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} P_i \times_{\pi_i} E \]

    This completes the construction of the functor \(G(E)\). On the other hand, we have a functorial homomorphism

    \[ \alpha \colon \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}(\pi)} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} FG \]

    i.e. a homomorphism functorial in the object \(E\) of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\):

    \[ \alpha(E) \colon E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} FG(E) = F(P \times_{\pi} E) \]

    namely the composite of the canonical morphisms

    \[ E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P) \times_{\pi} E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P \times_{\pi} E) \]

    (where the first comes from the marked point \(\varphi \in F(P)\)). Combining j) and k), we find:

  12. l)

    The homomorphism \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism

    One defines similarly a functorial homomorphism

    \[ \beta \colon GF \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}} \]

    i.e. a homomorphism functorial in the object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\):

    \[ \beta(X) \colon P \times_{\pi} F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \]

    as associated to the \(\pi\)-homomorphism

    \[ F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \]

    inverse of the canonical isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F(X)\).

  13. m)

    The composites

    \begin{gather*} F(X) \xrightarrow{\alpha(F(X))} FGF(X) \xrightarrow{F(\beta(X))} F(X)\\ G(E) \xrightarrow{G(\alpha(E))} GFG(E) \xrightarrow{\beta(G(E))} G(E) \end{gather*}

    are the identity isomorphisms.

    Routine verification.

    Taking into account l), it follows:

  14. n)

    The homomorphism \(\beta\) is an isomorphism

    We have thus obtained the promised result:

Theorem 1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category satisfying the conditions (G 1), (G 2), (G 3) from the beginning of the article, and \(F\) a covariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of finite sets, satisfying the conditions (G 4), (G 5) and (G 6). Then the preceding canonical constructions define equivalences of categories \(F\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}(\pi)\) and \(G\colon \mathscr{C}(\pi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}\) quasi-inverse to one another. More precisely, there exists a pro-object \(P\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), and a functorial isomorphism \(F(X) \overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P, X)\), \(\pi\) is the group opposite to the group of automorphisms of \(P\), suitably topologized, in such a way that \(\pi\) acts continuously on the sets \(\operatorname{Hom}(P, X)\simeq F(X)\). Finally \(G\) is given by \(G(E)\simeq P\times_{\pi} E\).

Remarks 2

The statement of the conditions (G 1) to (G 6) becomes simpler and more pleasant if one replaces (G 2) and (G 5) respectively by:

  1. (G' 2)

    Finite direct limits in \(\mathscr{C}\) exist.

  2. (G' 5)

    The functor \(F\) is right exact (i.e. commutes with finite direct limits).

These conditions are apparently stronger than (G 2) and (G 5), but it follows at once from the structure theorem 1 that they are entailed by (G 1) to (G 6). It will be noted, however, that in the cases which will interest us, the verification of (G 2) and (G 5) seems effectively simpler than that of (G' 2) and (G' 5). I do not know whether in condition (G 3), the fact that \(u''\) is an isomorphism onto a direct summand of \(Y\) could not be omitted.

blocks.html: 4 Axiomatic conditions for a Galois theory
Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category, \(F\) a covariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of finite sets. Suppose the following conditions satisfied:

1. (G 1)

    \(\mathscr{C}\) has a final object1 and the fibred product of two objects over a third in \(\mathscr{C}\) exists (this axiom may also be stated by saying that in \(\mathscr{C}\) finite inverse limits exist).

  2. (G 2)

    Finite sums in \(\mathscr{C}\) exist (hence also an initial object \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), playing the role of the empty set), as well as the quotient of an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) by a finite group of automorphisms.

  3. (G 3)

    Let \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism in \(\mathscr{C}\), then \(u\) factors as a product \(X\xrightarrow{u'} Y'\xrightarrow{u''} Y\), with \(u'\) a strict epimorphism and \(u''\) a monomorphism, which is an isomorphism onto a direct summand of \(Y\).

  4. (G 4)

    The functor \(F\) is left exact (i.e. transforms a right unit into a right unit, and commutes with fibred products).

  5. (G 5)

    \(F\) commutes with finite direct sums, transforms strict epimorphisms into epi­mor­phisms, and commutes with passage to the quotient by a finite group of automorphisms.

  6. (G 6)

    Let \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism in \(\mathscr{C}\) such that \(F(u)\) is an isomorphism, then \(u\) is an isomorphism.

Our object is to construct a topological group \(\pi\), inverse limit of finite groups, and an equivalence of the category \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) of finite sets on which \(\pi\) operates continuously (i.e. in such a way that the stabilizer of a point is an open subgroup, or equivalently that there exists a discrete quotient group which already operates on the set considered), the equivalence constructed transforming the given functor \(F\) into the obvious inclusion functor of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) into the category of finite sets. We note at once that the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) constructed by means of a topological group \(\pi\), and the preceding inclusion functor, do indeed satisfy conditions (G 1) to (G 6).
We proceed in several steps.

1. a)

    Let \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) in \(\mathscr{C}\). In order that \(u\) be a monomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(F(u)\) be so. (Uses (G 1), (G 4), (G 6)).

    Indeed, to say that \(u\) is a monomorphism means that the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_1 \colon X \times_Y X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is an isomorphism.

  2. b)

    Every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) is Artinian.

    Indeed, if \(X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) are monomorphisms such that \(F(X')\) and \(F(X'')\) have the same image in \(F(X)\), then by a) \(F(X') \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(X'')\) is an isomorphism, hence \(X' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X''\) is an isomorphism by (G 6).

  3. c)

    The functor \(F\) is strictly pro-representable. (cf. Grothendieck, Technique de descente et théorèmes d'existence en Géométrie Algébrique, II, Séminaire Bourbaki 195, février 1960).

    Indeed, according to loc. cit. prop. 1, this follows from b) and (G 4). We can therefore find an inverse system over the filtered ordered set \(I\):

    \[ P=(P_i)_{i \in I} \]

    in \(\mathscr{C}\), regarded as a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), and a functorial isomorphism

    \[ \tag{*} F(X)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\operatorname{Pro}(\mathscr{C})}(P,X)=\varinjlim_{i} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}}(P_i,X) \]

    This isomorphism is realized by an element

    \[ \varphi \in \varprojlim_{i} F(P_i)=F(P) \]

    We may moreover assume that the transition homomorphisms \(\varphi_{ji} \colon P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_j\) (\(i \geqslant j\)) are epimorphisms, and that every epimorphism \(P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P'\) is equivalent to an epimorphism \(P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_j\) for a suitable \(j \leqslant i\) (which determines the inverse system \(P\) in an essentially unique way).

    An object \(X \in \mathscr{C}\) is said to be connected if it is not isomorphic to the sum of two objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) not isomorphic to the initial object \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

  4. d)

    The \(P_i\) are connected and not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

    If \(X\) is a left unit, we have \(F(X)=\emptyset\) by (G 5) applied to the direct sum of an empty family, and conversely by (G 6). Hence if \(X'\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) which is not a left unit, i.e. such that \(F(X') \neq \emptyset\), there exists no morphism from \(X'\) to \(X\). Hence if some \(P_i\) is a left unit, then \(i\) is a greatest element of the filtered ordered index set \(I\), and formula (*) would mean \(F(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,X)={}\) a set reduced to a single element for every \(X\), which is absurd since \(F(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}})=\emptyset\). Hence the \(P_i\) are not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

    Suppose we have \(P_i=A \amalg B\), whence by (G 5) \(F(P_i)=F(A) \amalg F(B)\); in particular the element \(a_i\) of \(F(P_i)\), corresponding by (*) to the identity homomorphism \(P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\), lies in \(F(A) \amalg F(B)\), for instance in \(F(A)\). This means that there exists a \(j \geqslant i\) such that \(\varphi_{ij} \colon P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) factors through \(P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i=A \amalg B\), where the second arrow is the canonical morphism. Hence \(F(P_j) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P_i)\) factors through \(F(P_j) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(A) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P_i)=F(A) \amalg F(B)\), and since \(F(P_j) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P_i)\) is surjective by (G 5), it follows that \(F(B)=\emptyset\), hence \(B\) is isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\).

  5. e)

    Every morphism \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) in \(\mathscr{C}\), with \(X\) not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) and \(Y\) connected, is a strict epimorphism. Every endomorphism of a connected object is an automorphism.

    Consider the factorization (G 3) of \(u\); since \(X \neq \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), it follows from (G 6) that \(F(X) \neq \emptyset\), hence \(F(Y') \neq \emptyset\), hence \(Y' \neq \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), so, \(Y\) being connected, \(Y'\) is identified with \(Y\), hence \(u\) is a strict epimorphism. Suppose that \(u\) is an endomorphism of the connected object \(X\); let us prove that it is an automorphism. Indeed, we may assume \(X\) not isomorphic to \(\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\), so \(u\) is a strict epimorphism by what precedes, hence \(F(u)\) is an epimorphism by (G 5), and since \(F(X)\) is a finite set, \(F(u)\) is bijective. Hence \(u\) is an automorphism by (G 6).

    In particular, every endomorphism of a \(P_i\) is an automorphism.

  6. f)

    The following conditions on a \(P_i\) are equivalent: (i) The natural injective map \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i) \simeq F(P_i)\) is also surjective, i.e. for every \(u \colon P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) there exists a \(v \colon P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) such that \(u=v\varphi_i\) (where \(\varphi_i\) is the canonical homomorphism \(P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\)). (ii) The group \(\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\) operates transitively on \(F(P_i)\). (iii) The group \(\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\) operates simply transitively on \(F(P_i)\).

    Indeed, identifying \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i)\) with \(F(P_i)\), the map considered in (i) is none other than \(v \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F(v)(\varphi_i)\). The equivalence of the three conditions then comes from the fact that \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i)=\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\) and that the preceding map is already injective.

    A \(P_i\) satisfying the equivalent conditions (i), (ii), (iii) of f) is called Galois.

  7. g)

    For every \(X\) in \(\mathscr{C}\), there exists a \(P_i\) Galois such that every \(u \in \operatorname{Hom}(P,X)\) factors through \(P \xrightarrow{\varphi_i} P_i \longrightarrow X\).

    Let \(J=\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)=F(X)\); this is a finite set, so there exists a \(P_j\) such that every \(u \colon P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) factors through \(P\xrightarrow{j}P_j\longrightarrow X\), or equivalently such that the natural morphism

    \[ P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J \qquad (J=\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) \]

    factors through

    \[ P \xrightarrow{\varphi_j} P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J \]

    By virtue of (G 3), the morphism \(P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J\) factors as the product of a monomorphism by a strict epimorphism, which one may take to be of the form \(\varphi_{ij} \colon P_j \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\). We are thus reduced to proving that \(P_i\) is Galois. Let \(k\) be an index \(\geqslant j\) such that every morphism \(P \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) factors through \(P \xrightarrow{\varphi_k} P_k \longrightarrow P_i\). Note that the natural morphism \(P_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X^J\) again factors as the composite

    \[ P_k \xrightarrow{\varphi_{ik}} P_i \xrightarrow{U} X^J \]

    where the first arrow is a strict epimorphism by e), and the second a monomorphism. We wish to prove that for a given morphism \(\psi \colon P_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\), there exists an endomorphism \(v\) of \(P_i\) such that \(\psi = v \varphi_{ik}\). But for every \(u \in \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,X)\), consider \(u \psi \in \operatorname{Hom}(P_k,X)\); it is therefore of the form \(u'\varphi_{ik}\) with a well-determined \(u' \in \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,X)\). The map \(u \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} u'\) from \(J\) to \(J\) thus defined by \(\psi\) is moreover injective, since \(\psi\) is an epimorphism by virtue of e); it is therefore bijective since the set \(J\) is finite. The bijective map \(u \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} u'\) from \(J\) to \(J\) thus defines an isomorphism \(\alpha \colon X^J \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} X^J\) making the diagram commutative

    \[ \xymatrix{{P_k}\ar[r]^-{\varphi_{ik}}\ar@{=}[d]&{P_i}\ar[r]^-U&{X^J}\ar[d]^-{\alpha}_-{\simeq}\\{P_k}\ar[r]^{\psi}&{P_i}\ar[r]^-U&{X^J}} \]

    By the uniqueness properties of the factorization of a morphism as the product of a monomorphism by a strict epimorphism, it follows (since \(\psi\) too is a strict epimorphism by e)) that one can find a morphism \(v \colon P_i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i\) which keeps the diagram commutative, q.e.d.

    We conclude in particular that the Galois \(P_i\) form a cofinal system in the system of the \((P_j)\). We shall therefore have, since for a Galois object \(P_i\) we have

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i)=\operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i)=\operatorname{Aut}(P_i), \]

    by passage to the limit:

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}(P,P) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Aut}(P_i) \]

    where .5cm the inverse limit is taken over the Galois \(P_i\). Moreover, by means of the identification \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i)=F(P_i)\), and taking into account that \(F\) transforms epimorphisms into epimorphisms, we see that the transition homomorphisms in the preceding inverse system are surjective. From all this we conclude:

  8. h)

    We have

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}(P,P) = \operatorname{Aut}(P) = \varprojlim_{i} F(P_i) = \varprojlim_{i} \operatorname{Aut}(P_i), \]

    where the inverse limit is taken over the \(P_i\) Galois.

    In particular, \(\operatorname{Aut}(P)\) appears as the inverse limit of an inverse system of finite groups (the transition homomorphisms being surjective); we shall endow it with the inverse limit topology of the discrete topologies. We shall denote by \(\pi\) and shall call the fundamental group (of \(\mathscr{C}\) equipped with \(F\)) the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(P)\). This group therefore operates on the right on \(P\); it is the inverse limit of finite groups \(\pi_i\) operating on the right on the Galois \(P_i\), \(\pi_i\) being the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(P_i)\).

    Taking into account the functorial isomorphism

    \[ F(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \]

    and the definition of \(\pi\), we thus see that \(\pi\) operates on the left on \(F(X)\), and moreover continuously by g) (for, with the notation of g), it is in fact \(\pi_i\) that operates on \(F(X)\)). It is trivial that for every morphism \(u \colon X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) in \(\mathscr{C}\), the morphism \(F(u) \colon F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(Y)\) is compatible with the operations of \(\pi\). We may therefore henceforth regard \(F\) as a covariant functor

    \[ F \colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}(\pi) \]

    where \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) is the category of finite sets on which \(\pi\) operates continuously on the left.

    We shall now define a functor in the opposite direction:

    \[ G \colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longleftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow}{\leftarrow} \mathscr{C}(\pi) \]

    by the formula

    \[ G(E)=P \times_{\pi} E, \]

    where \(P \times_{\pi} E\) is defined as the solution of the universal problem summarized by

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathscr{C}}(P \times_{\pi} E,X) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) \]

    (where in the second member \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)=F(X)\) is regarded as a set on which \(\pi\) operates on the left). We must prove the existence of the object \(P \times_{\pi} E\).

  9. i)

    Let \(Q\) be an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) on which a finite group \(G\) operates on the right, and \(E\) a finite set on which \(G\) operates on the left. Then \(G \times_G E\) exists, and the canonical map

    \[ F(Q) \times_G E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(Q \times_G E) \]

    is an isomorphism.

    Since finite direct sums exist in \(\mathscr{C}\) by (G 2), and \(F\) commutes with them by (G 5), we are immediately reduced to the case where \(G\) operates transitively on \(E\); for if the \(E_j\) are the orbits of \(G\) in \(E\), we shall have

    \[ Q \times_G E = \underset {j}{\amalg}\ Q \times_G E_j. \]

    Let then \(a \in E\), let \(H\) be its stabilizer; one sees at once from the definition that \(Q \times_G E\) is identified with \(Q/H\). Whence the existence, thanks to (G 2), and the commutation property for \(F\), thanks to (G 5).

  10. j)

    Let \(E\) be an object of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), and let \(P_i\) be Galois such that \(\pi_i\) already operates on \(E\). Then \(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E\) exists and we have a canonical isomorphism

    \[ E \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E) \]

    If \(j \geqslant i\) is such that \(P_j\) is Galois, then the canonical homomorphism \(P_j \times_{\pi_j} E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P_i \times_{\pi_i} E\) is an isomorphism.

    The first assertion is a special case of i), taking into account that \(\pi_i\) operates simply transitively on \(F(P_i)\), which is equipped with a marked point \(\varphi_i\), whence an isomorphism \(F(P_i) \times_{\pi_i} E \simeq E\). For the second assertion one uses for example (G 6).

    For every \(j\), let \(\mathscr{C}_j\) be the full subcategory of \(\mathscr{C}\) formed by the \(X\) such that \(\operatorname{Hom}(P_j,X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \simeq F(X)\) is bijective. We know by g) that \(\mathscr{C}\) is the filtered union of the \(\mathscr{C}_j\). We therefore have for \(X \in \mathscr{C}_j\):

    \begin{align*} \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) & \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P_j,X)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi_j}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P_j,X))\\ & \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(P_j \times_{\pi_j} E,X) \end{align*}

    and taking into account the last assertion in j), we find an isomorphism functorial in the object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}_j\):

    \[ \operatorname{Hom}_{\pi}(E,\operatorname{Hom}(P,X)) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E,X) \]

    Since this is true for every \(j\) and these functorial isomorphisms, for varying \(j\), induce one another, we conclude:

  11. k)

    Under the conditions of j), the morphism composed of the canonical morphisms

    \[ E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P_i,P_i \times_{\pi_i} E) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,P_i \times_{\pi_i} E) \]

    makes of \(P_i \times_{\pi_i} E\) a solution of the universal problem defining \(P \times_{\pi} E\), i.e. the latter exists and we have an isomorphism

    \[ P \times_{\pi} E \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} P_i \times_{\pi_i} E \]

    This completes the construction of the functor \(G(E)\). On the other hand, we have a functorial homomorphism

    \[ \alpha \colon \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}(\pi)} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} FG \]

    i.e. a homomorphism functorial in the object \(E\) of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\):

    \[ \alpha(E) \colon E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} FG(E) = F(P \times_{\pi} E) \]

    namely the composite of the canonical morphisms

    \[ E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P) \times_{\pi} E \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(P \times_{\pi} E) \]

    (where the first comes from the marked point \(\varphi \in F(P)\)). Combining j) and k), we find:

  12. l)

    The homomorphism \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism

    One defines similarly a functorial homomorphism

    \[ \beta \colon GF \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathrm{id}_{\mathscr{C}} \]

    i.e. a homomorphism functorial in the object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\):

    \[ \beta(X) \colon P \times_{\pi} F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \]

    as associated to the \(\pi\)-homomorphism

    \[ F(X) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \]

    inverse of the canonical isomorphism \(\operatorname{Hom}(P,X) \overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} F(X)\).

  13. m)

    The composites

    \begin{gather*} F(X) \xrightarrow{\alpha(F(X))} FGF(X) \xrightarrow{F(\beta(X))} F(X)\\ G(E) \xrightarrow{G(\alpha(E))} GFG(E) \xrightarrow{\beta(G(E))} G(E) \end{gather*}

    are the identity isomorphisms.

    Routine verification.

    Taking into account l), it follows:

  14. n)

    The homomorphism \(\beta\) is an isomorphism

    We have thus obtained the promised result:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a category satisfying the conditions (G 1), (G 2), (G 3) from the beginning of the article, and \(F\) a covariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of finite sets, satisfying the conditions (G 4), (G 5) and (G 6). Then the preceding canonical constructions define equivalences of categories \(F\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}(\pi)\) and \(G\colon \mathscr{C}(\pi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}\) quasi-inverse to one another. More precisely, there exists a pro-object \(P\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), and a functorial isomorphism \(F(X) \overset{\sim}{\longleftarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P, X)\), \(\pi\) is the group opposite to the group of automorphisms of \(P\), suitably topologized, in such a way that \(\pi\) acts continuously on the sets \(\operatorname{Hom}(P, X)\simeq F(X)\). Finally \(G\) is given by \(G(E)\simeq P\times_{\pi} E\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2

The statement of the conditions (G 1) to (G 6) becomes simpler and more pleasant if one replaces (G 2) and (G 5) respectively by:

  1. (G' 2)

    Finite direct limits in \(\mathscr{C}\) exist.

  2. (G' 5)

    The functor \(F\) is right exact (i.e. commutes with finite direct limits).

These conditions are apparently stronger than (G 2) and (G 5), but it follows at once from the structure theorem 1 that they are entailed by (G 1) to (G 6). It will be noted, however, that in the cases which will interest us, the verification of (G 2) and (G 5) seems effectively simpler than that of (G' 2) and (G' 5). I do not know whether in condition (G 3), the fact that \(u''\) is an isomorphism onto a direct summand of \(Y\) could not be omitted.

footnotes.html: Let us recall that an object \(e\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) is called a final object if for every \(X\) in \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\operatorname{Hom}(X,e)\) has exactly one element. One defines dually an initial object of \(\mathscr{C}\).

pages.title: Galois categories
pages.html:
5 Galois categories

Definition 1

A Galois category is a category \(\mathscr{C}\) equivalent to a category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), where \(\pi\) is a compact group, inverse limit of finite groups (i.e. totally disconnected).

For the definition of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), cf. the beginning of 4. By virtue of Th. 1, \(\mathscr{C}\) is Galois if and only if it satisfies conditions (G 1) to (G 3), and if there exists a functor \(F\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of finite sets satisfying conditions (G 4) to (G 6) (i.e. which is exact and conservative, in a general terminology). Such a functor will be called a fundamental functor of the Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\)1; it is pro-representable by a pro-object which we shall denote \(P_F\); a pro-object \(P\) such that the associated functor \(F\) is fundamental is called a fundamental pro-object. In this way, the category of fundamental functors on \(\mathscr{C}\) is anti-equivalent to the category of fundamental pro-objects; if \(F\) and \(P\) correspond to each other, the group \(\operatorname{Aut} F\) is therefore isomorphic to the opposite of the group \(\operatorname{Aut} P\), so that the group denoted \(\pi\) in the preceding article is none other than \(\operatorname{Aut} P\). Let us recall that in the preceding article we constructed, starting from a given fundamental functor \(F\), an equivalence of \(\mathscr{C}\) with \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) (where \(\pi=\operatorname{Aut}(F)\)) which transforms the functor \(F\) into the canonical functor from \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)_x\) into the category of finite sets. In this typical case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), \(F=\) canonical functor, the fundamental pro-object associated with \(F\) is none other than the inverse system of the discrete quotients \(\pi_i\) of \(\pi\).

It may be useful to make explicit the category of pro-objects of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\). One finds:

Proposition 2

The category \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) is canonically equivalent to the category \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) of spaces, with topological group \(\pi\) of operators, which are compact and totally disconnected.

Since this latter category contains \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) as a full subcategory (corresponding to the spaces with discrete compact operators) and since inverse limits exist in it, one has in any case a canonical functor \(g\colon \operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}(\pi) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}'(\pi)\), to the inverse system \(Q=(Q_i)\) corresponding the object \(X=\varprojlim_{i} Q_i\) of \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\). To define a functor in the reverse direction, it suffices to define a contravariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) into the category of left exact functors \(\mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Ens}\), and one will take for every \(X\in\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) the functor \(h(X)(E)= \operatorname{Hom}(X, E)\) (the \(\operatorname{Hom}\) being taken in \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\)). It is immediate by definition that the functors \(h\) and \(g\) are adjoint to one another, and that \(hg\) is canonically isomorphic to the identity functor of \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}(\pi)\). It remains to prove (in order to establish that \(g\) and \(h\) are quasi-inverse to each other) that every object of \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(g(Q)\), where \(Q\in\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}{\mathscr{C}}(\pi)\), in other words: every space \(X\) with topological group \(\pi\) of operators, which is compact and totally disconnected, is isomorphic to an inverse limit of spaces with finite discrete operators. Since \(X\) is the inverse limit of its finite discrete quotients (as a topological space without operators), one is reduced to showing that among the set of these quotients, there is a cofinal system which is invariant under \(\pi\). For this it suffices to show that for such a quotient \(X'\), the set of transforms of this quotient by the operations of \(\pi\) is finite, (one will then take the sup of the said transforms, which will be an invariant quotient majorizing \(X'\)). Or again, that there is an open invariant subgroup \(\pi'\) of \(\pi\) such that the elements of this subgroup leave \(X'\) invariant. Now \(X'\) corresponds to a finite partition of \(X\) into open sets \(X_i\). By reason of the continuity and the compactness of \(\pi\), there exists a neighbourhood \(V\) of the neutral element of \(\pi\) such that \(s\in V\) implies \(s\cdot X_i\subset X_i\) for every \(i\), so that \(s\) leaves \(X'\) invariant. Now it is known that the open invariant subgroups of \(\pi\) form a fundamental system of neighbourhoods of the neutral element, which completes the proof.

Let us remark that one sees still more simply that the category \(\operatorname{Ind} \mathscr{C}(\pi)\) is canonically equivalent to the category of sets on which \(\pi\) operates continuously. We shall not need this here.

Proposition 3

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category, \(F\) a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C}\), \(P=(P_i)\) the associated pro-object, normalized in the usual way. Let \(X\in\mathscr{C}\); for \(X\) to be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\pi\) operate transitively on \(E=F(X)\).

One is reduced to the typical case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), \(F=\) canonical functor, where it is trivial.

Corollary 4

Equivalent conditions on \(X\): (i) \(X\) is connected and \(\not\simeq\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) (ii) the group \(\pi\) is transitive on \(E=F(X)\), and \(F(X)\ne\emptyset\) (iii) \(X\) is isomorphic to a \(P_i\).

The equivalence of (i) and (iii) also already follows easily from 4, e).

Proposition 5

Let \(Q=(Q_i)_{i\in I}\) be a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), normalized in the usual way, and let \(G\) be the corresponding functor \(G(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(Q, X)\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) (into \(\mathbf{Ens}\)). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(G\) commutes with finite direct sums

  2. (ii)

    \(G\) commutes with the sum of two objects

  3. (iii)

    The \(Q_i\) are connected and \(\not\simeq\emptyset_\mathscr{C}\)

  4. (iv)

    \(Q\) is isomorphic to \(\pi/H\), where \(H\) is a closed subgroup of \(\pi\).

  5. (v)

    The functor \(G\) is isomorphic to the functor \(E\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E^H\) (set of invariants under \(H\)) defined by a closed subgroup \(H\) of \(\pi\).

N.B. in the statement of (iv) and (v), one supposes chosen a fundamental functor, allowing one to identify \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\).

Proof

One may suppose \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\). The implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is trivial, (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) is proved like property d) of 4. Let us prove (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv). Indeed, one knows that \(\varprojlim_i Q_i\) is non-empty as an inverse limit of non-empty finite sets. Let \(a\) be a point of \(\varprojlim_i Q_i\); it defines a homomorphism of spaces with operators

\[ \pi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q \]

which is surjective, since for every \(i\) the composite \(\pi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q_i\) is so, since \(\pi\) is transitive in \(Q_i\) by virtue of 3. If then \(H\) is the stabilizer subgroup of \(a\), one obtains an isomorphism \(\pi/H\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} Q\). The implications (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(v) and (v)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) are again trivial.

Proposition 6

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category, \(P\) a fundamental pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(F\) the associated fundamental functor. Let \(P'=(P'_i)_{i\in I}\) be a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), put into normal form, and \(F'\) the associated functor \(F'(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(P',X)\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathbf{Ens}\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(P'\simeq P\), or again \(F'\simeq F\).

  2. (ii)

    \(P'\) is fundamental, or again \(F'\) is fundamental.

  3. (iii)

    \(F'\) transforms the sum of two objects into a sum, and \(X\not\simeq\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) implies \(F(X)\ne\emptyset\).

  4. (iv)

    The objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) that are connected and \(\ne\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) are exactly the objects isomorphic to a \(P'_i\).

We trivially have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) and (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), moreover (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) by virtue of 4 (applied to \(P'\) instead of \(P\)). Moreover (iii) or (iv) implies by virtue of 5 that \(P'\) is of the form \(\pi/H\) where \(H\) is a closed subgroup of \(\pi\). In case (iii), there exists for every open invariant subgroup \(\pi'\) of \(\pi\) a \(\pi\)-homomorphism \(P'=\pi/H\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi/\pi'\), whence \(H\subset \pi'\), whence \(H=(0)\) and consequently (i), q.e.d.

Corollary 7

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category. The fundamental pro-objects are isomorphic, the fundamental functors are isomorphic.

In other terms, the category of fundamental functors is a connected groupoid \(\Gamma\), which may be called the fundamental groupoid of the Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\). If \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), the group of automorphisms of an object of the fundamental groupoid is isomorphic to \(\pi\), this isomorphism being well determined up to inner automorphism. (N.B. one calls groupoid a category in which all the morphisms are isomorphisms, connected groupoid a groupoid all of whose objects are isomorphic). The fundamental pro-objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) form a connected groupoid equivalent to the opposite of the fundamental groupoid. If \(F, F'\) are two fundamental functors, associated with fundamental pro-objects \(P, P'\), then \(\operatorname{Hom}(F, F')=\operatorname{Isom}(F, F')\) is sometimes denoted \(\pi_{F', F}\) and plays the role of a “set of path classes from \(F\) to \(F'\)”. In particular \(\pi_{F, F}=\pi_F\) is none other than the fundamental group of \(\mathscr{C}\) at \(F\) constructed in the preceding number. As for the pro-object \(P\) associated with \(F\), it plays the role of a universal covering at \(F\) of the final object \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) of \(\mathscr{C}\).

It may be convenient to have a description of \(\mathscr{C}\) (up to equivalence) in terms of its fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\), without passing through a choice of a particular object \(F\) of the latter. Now to every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) is associated the functor \(E_X\) on the fundamental groupoid, defined by

\[ E_X(F)=F(X), \]

with values in \(\mathbf{Ens}\). (Such a functor is known in topology under the name of “local system” on the groupoid) \(F(X)=E_X(F)\) may be called the fibre of \(X\) at \(F\), and the functor \(E_X\) the fibre-functor associated with \(X\). The functor \(E_X\) has the following property: for every \(F\), \(E_X(F)\) is a finite set on which the topological group \(\pi_F=\operatorname{Aut}(F)\) operates continuously. For a given covariant functor \(\xi\) of the fundamental groupoid into \(\mathbf{Ens}\), the preceding condition is moreover equivalent to the same condition for one fixed arbitrary \(F\). This being posited:

Proposition 8

The functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\) is an equivalence of the category \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category of covariant functors of the fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathbf{Ens}\), which satisfy the condition brought to light above.

Indeed, let \(F_0\) be an object of the fundamental groupoid, and let \(\pi_0=\operatorname{Aut}(F_0)\), then the functor \(\xi\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \xi(F_0)\) is an equivalence of the second category considered in 8 with the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi_0)\), as one observes at once. On the other hand, the composite of the latter and of the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\) is the natural equivalence \(\mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}(\pi_0)\). It follows that the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\) itself is an equivalence.

Corollary 9

The category \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}\) is canonically equivalent to the category of covariant functors \(\xi\) of the fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\) into the category of topological spaces, satisfying the condition: for every object \(F\) of \(\Gamma\), \(\xi(F)\) is a compact totally disconnected space with topological group \(\pi_F\) of operators.

Here again, one may verify this condition on \(\xi\); it suffices to verify it for one \(F\). The proof is the same as for 8.

Remark 10

Let \((F_s)_{s\in S}\) be a family of objects of the fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\). Set for \(s, s'\in S\):

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(s, s')=\operatorname{Hom}(F_s, F_{s'}) \]

so that \(S\) itself becomes a connected groupoid and the map \(s\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F_s\) a fully faithful functor of \(S\) into \(\Gamma\), say \(f\). Considering then the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\circ f\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(S,\mathbf{Ens})\), one obtains a variant of 8 (and 9) with \(\Gamma\) replaced by \(S\). The statement thus obtained reduces to theorem 1 when \(S\) is reduced to a point, and is none other than 8 itself if \(S\) is the set of objects of \(\Gamma\).

We are going to use 9 to define a canonical pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\). For this, we consider the functor of \(\Gamma\) into the category of topological spaces (and even of topological groups):

\[ f\colon F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{Aut}(F)=\pi_F. \]

This functor satisfies the condition considered in 8, the space with operators \(f(F)\) under \(\pi_F\) is none other than \(\pi_F\), considered as a space with operators under itself by the inner automorphisms. Hence the functor \(f\) corresponds to a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\) determined up to unique isomorphism, which is even a pro-group of \(\mathscr{C}\) and which is called the fundamental pro-group of \({\mathscr{C}}\), playing the role of a local system of fundamental groups. It is thus a pro-group \(\Pi\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) defined by the condition that one have an isomorphism functorial in \(F\)

\[ F(\Pi)\simeq\pi_F. \]

If \(X\) is an arbitrary pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), one has a canonical morphism

\[ \Pi\times X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \]

which makes \(X\) an object with left operator group \(G\) in \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}\). It suffices for this to note that for \(F\) variable, one has a canonical map

\[ \Pi(F)\times X(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X(F) \]

i.e.

\[ \operatorname{Aut}(F)\times E_X(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_X(F), \quad \text{or} \quad \pi_F\times F(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(X) \]

which is functorial in \(F\). It is also functorial in \(X\), hence for every morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of pro-objects, the corresponding diagram

\[ \xymatrix{{\Pi \times X}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{X}\ar[d]\\{\Pi \times Y}\ar[r]&{Y}} \]

is commutative.

Remark 11

One must take care not to confuse a fundamental pro-object \(P\) (which is not endowed with a group structure, and is connected) with the fundamental pro-group (which is a pro-group, and in general not connected). More precisely, \(G\) is connected if and only if \(\pi_F\) acting on itself by inner automorphisms is transitive, i.e. if \(\pi\) is reduced to the identity element, or again if \(\mathscr{C}\) is equivalent to the category of finite sets. Another essential difference is that \(G\) is determined up to unique isomorphism, while \(P\) is only determined up to non-unique isomorphism.

Let \(E\) be a finite set and consider the constant functor on the groupoid \(\Gamma\), with value \(E\): by virtue of 8 it defines an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), denoted \(E_{\mathscr{C}}\), and which may also be interpreted as the sum of \(E\) copies of the final object \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) of \(\mathscr{C}\). One may regard \(E_{\mathscr{C}}\) as a functor in \(E\), from the category of finite sets to the category \(\mathscr{C}\), and this functor is exact, hence transforms finite groups into \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups, etc. If therefore \(X\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) on which the finite group \(G\) acts on the right, one sees that one may regard \(X\) as an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) having a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group of operators on the right \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\). One will therefore say, by extension of a general terminology relative to objects with \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups of operators, that \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G\) if \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\), i.e. if the canonical morphism

\[ X\times G_{\mathscr{C}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times X \]

deduced from the operation of \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\) on \(X\) on the right, is an isomorphism. One says that \(X\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\) if it is so under \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\), i.e. if it is so formally, and if moreover the quotient \(X/G=X/G_{\mathscr{C}}\) is \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\). If one fixes a fundamental functor, whence an equivalence of \(\mathscr{C}\) with a category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), \(X\) corresponds to a set on which \(\pi\) acts on the left continuously, say \(E=F(X)\). To make \(G\) act on \(X\) on the right then amounts to making \(G\) act on the set \(E\) on the right, in such a way that the operations of \(G\) commute with those of \(\pi\). One then observes at once that \(X\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\) if and only if the set \(E\) is a principal homogeneous space under \(G\), i.e. if and only if \(G\) acts on it in a simply transitive way. (Moreover \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous \(E\) principal homogeneous or empty). Comparing with 3, one sees that if \(X\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\) and connected, then the given homomorphism from \(G\) to the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(X)\) is an isomorphism; and moreover, for an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) to be connected and principal homogeneous under the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(X)\), it is necessary and sufficient, with the notations of 4, that it be isomorphic to a Galois \(P_i\). In the typical case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), this means that \(X\) is isomorphic to a quotient of \(\pi\) by an invariant subgroup.

Suppose still given a fundamental functor \(F\). Then the datum of an \(X\) principal homogeneous under a finite group \(G\) acting on the right, and of a point \(a\in F(X)\), is equivalent to the datum of a homomorphism from \(\pi\) to the group \(G\). Indeed, to such a homomorphism one associates the set \(E=G\), making \(\pi\) act on it on the left by means of the given homomorphism \(\pi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) and the left translations of \(G\), and making \(G\) act on it on the right by right translation, the marked point \(a\) of \(E\) being the identity element of \(G\). Thanks to what precedes, one thus obtains in an essentially unique way every triple \((X, G, a)\) having the properties envisaged above, since a set with a marked point that is principal homogeneous under a group \(G\) is identified with the latter. In this way, one has a direct geometric interpretation of the functor \(G\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{Hom}(\pi, G)\) from the category of finite groups to \(\mathbf{Ens}\), a functor which is pro-representable by means of \(\pi\), and whose consideration would thus furnish another construction of the group \(\pi\) associated to \(F\).
