blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1

A Galois category is a category \(\mathscr{C}\) equivalent to a category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), where \(\pi\) is a compact group, inverse limit of finite groups (i.e. totally disconnected).

For the definition of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), cf. the beginning of 4. By virtue of Th. 1, \(\mathscr{C}\) is Galois if and only if it satisfies conditions (G 1) to (G 3), and if there exists a functor \(F\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of finite sets satisfying conditions (G 4) to (G 6) (i.e. which is exact and conservative, in a general terminology). Such a functor will be called a fundamental functor of the Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\)1; it is pro-representable by a pro-object which we shall denote \(P_F\); a pro-object \(P\) such that the associated functor \(F\) is fundamental is called a fundamental pro-object. In this way, the category of fundamental functors on \(\mathscr{C}\) is anti-equivalent to the category of fundamental pro-objects; if \(F\) and \(P\) correspond to each other, the group \(\operatorname{Aut} F\) is therefore isomorphic to the opposite of the group \(\operatorname{Aut} P\), so that the group denoted \(\pi\) in the preceding article is none other than \(\operatorname{Aut} P\). Let us recall that in the preceding article we constructed, starting from a given fundamental functor \(F\), an equivalence of \(\mathscr{C}\) with \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) (where \(\pi=\operatorname{Aut}(F)\)) which transforms the functor \(F\) into the canonical functor from \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)_x\) into the category of finite sets. In this typical case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), \(F=\) canonical functor, the fundamental pro-object associated with \(F\) is none other than the inverse system of the discrete quotients \(\pi_i\) of \(\pi\).
It may be useful to make explicit the category of pro-objects of \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\). One finds:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2

The category \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) is canonically equivalent to the category \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) of spaces, with topological group \(\pi\) of operators, which are compact and totally disconnected.

Since this latter category contains \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\) as a full subcategory (corresponding to the spaces with discrete compact operators) and since inverse limits exist in it, one has in any case a canonical functor \(g\colon \operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}(\pi) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}'(\pi)\), to the inverse system \(Q=(Q_i)\) corresponding the object \(X=\varprojlim_{i} Q_i\) of \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\). To define a functor in the reverse direction, it suffices to define a contravariant functor from \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) into the category of left exact functors \(\mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathbf{Ens}\), and one will take for every \(X\in\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) the functor \(h(X)(E)= \operatorname{Hom}(X, E)\) (the \(\operatorname{Hom}\) being taken in \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\)). It is immediate by definition that the functors \(h\) and \(g\) are adjoint to one another, and that \(hg\) is canonically isomorphic to the identity functor of \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}(\pi)\). It remains to prove (in order to establish that \(g\) and \(h\) are quasi-inverse to each other) that every object of \(\mathscr{C}'(\pi)\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(g(Q)\), where \(Q\in\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}{\mathscr{C}}(\pi)\), in other words: every space \(X\) with topological group \(\pi\) of operators, which is compact and totally disconnected, is isomorphic to an inverse limit of spaces with finite discrete operators. Since \(X\) is the inverse limit of its finite discrete quotients (as a topological space without operators), one is reduced to showing that among the set of these quotients, there is a cofinal system which is invariant under \(\pi\). For this it suffices to show that for such a quotient \(X'\), the set of transforms of this quotient by the operations of \(\pi\) is finite, (one will then take the sup of the said transforms, which will be an invariant quotient majorizing \(X'\)). Or again, that there is an open invariant subgroup \(\pi'\) of \(\pi\) such that the elements of this subgroup leave \(X'\) invariant. Now \(X'\) corresponds to a finite partition of \(X\) into open sets \(X_i\). By reason of the continuity and the compactness of \(\pi\), there exists a neighbourhood \(V\) of the neutral element of \(\pi\) such that \(s\in V\) implies \(s\cdot X_i\subset X_i\) for every \(i\), so that \(s\) leaves \(X'\) invariant. Now it is known that the open invariant subgroups of \(\pi\) form a fundamental system of neighbourhoods of the neutral element, which completes the proof.

Let us remark that one sees still more simply that the category \(\operatorname{Ind} \mathscr{C}(\pi)\) is canonically equivalent to the category of sets on which \(\pi\) operates continuously. We shall not need this here.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category, \(F\) a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C}\), \(P=(P_i)\) the associated pro-object, normalized in the usual way. Let \(X\in\mathscr{C}\); for \(X\) to be connected, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\pi\) operate transitively on \(E=F(X)\).
One is reduced to the typical case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), \(F=\) canonical functor, where it is trivial.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Equivalent conditions on \(X\): (i) \(X\) is connected and \(\not\simeq\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) (ii) the group \(\pi\) is transitive on \(E=F(X)\), and \(F(X)\ne\emptyset\) (iii) \(X\) is isomorphic to a \(P_i\).
The equivalence of (i) and (iii) also already follows easily from 4, e).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5

Let \(Q=(Q_i)_{i\in I}\) be a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), normalized in the usual way, and let \(G\) be the corresponding functor \(G(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(Q, X)\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) (into \(\mathbf{Ens}\)). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(G\) commutes with finite direct sums

  2. (ii)

    \(G\) commutes with the sum of two objects

  3. (iii)

    The \(Q_i\) are connected and \(\not\simeq\emptyset_\mathscr{C}\)

  4. (iv)

    \(Q\) is isomorphic to \(\pi/H\), where \(H\) is a closed subgroup of \(\pi\).

  5. (v)

    The functor \(G\) is isomorphic to the functor \(E\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E^H\) (set of invariants under \(H\)) defined by a closed subgroup \(H\) of \(\pi\).
N.B. in the statement of (iv) and (v), one supposes chosen a fundamental functor, allowing one to identify \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\).
Proof

One may suppose \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\). The implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is trivial, (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) is proved like property d) of 4. Let us prove (iii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv). Indeed, one knows that \(\varprojlim_i Q_i\) is non-empty as an inverse limit of non-empty finite sets. Let \(a\) be a point of \(\varprojlim_i Q_i\); it defines a homomorphism of spaces with operators
\[ \pi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q \]

which is surjective, since for every \(i\) the composite \(\pi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q_i\) is so, since \(\pi\) is transitive in \(Q_i\) by virtue of 3. If then \(H\) is the stabilizer subgroup of \(a\), one obtains an isomorphism \(\pi/H\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} Q\). The implications (iv)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(v) and (v)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(i) are again trivial.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category, \(P\) a fundamental pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\) and \(F\) the associated fundamental functor. Let \(P'=(P'_i)_{i\in I}\) be a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), put into normal form, and \(F'\) the associated functor \(F'(X)=\operatorname{Hom}(P',X)\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathbf{Ens}\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(P'\simeq P\), or again \(F'\simeq F\).

  2. (ii)

    \(P'\) is fundamental, or again \(F'\) is fundamental.

  3. (iii)

    \(F'\) transforms the sum of two objects into a sum, and \(X\not\simeq\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) implies \(F(X)\ne\emptyset\).

  4. (iv)

    The objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) that are connected and \(\ne\emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) are exactly the objects isomorphic to a \(P'_i\).

We trivially have (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iii) and (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii), moreover (ii)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(iv) by virtue of 4 (applied to \(P'\) instead of \(P\)). Moreover (iii) or (iv) implies by virtue of 5 that \(P'\) is of the form \(\pi/H\) where \(H\) is a closed subgroup of \(\pi\). In case (iii), there exists for every open invariant subgroup \(\pi'\) of \(\pi\) a \(\pi\)-homomorphism \(P'=\pi/H\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi/\pi'\), whence \(H\subset \pi'\), whence \(H=(0)\) and consequently (i), q.e.d.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category. The fundamental pro-objects are isomorphic, the fundamental functors are isomorphic.

In other terms, the category of fundamental functors is a connected groupoid \(\Gamma\), which may be called the fundamental groupoid of the Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\). If \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), the group of automorphisms of an object of the fundamental groupoid is isomorphic to \(\pi\), this isomorphism being well determined up to inner automorphism. (N.B. one calls groupoid a category in which all the morphisms are isomorphisms, connected groupoid a groupoid all of whose objects are isomorphic). The fundamental pro-objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) form a connected groupoid equivalent to the opposite of the fundamental groupoid. If \(F, F'\) are two fundamental functors, associated with fundamental pro-objects \(P, P'\), then \(\operatorname{Hom}(F, F')=\operatorname{Isom}(F, F')\) is sometimes denoted \(\pi_{F', F}\) and plays the role of a “set of path classes from \(F\) to \(F'\)”. In particular \(\pi_{F, F}=\pi_F\) is none other than the fundamental group of \(\mathscr{C}\) at \(F\) constructed in the preceding number. As for the pro-object \(P\) associated with \(F\), it plays the role of a universal covering at \(F\) of the final object \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) of \(\mathscr{C}\).

It may be convenient to have a description of \(\mathscr{C}\) (up to equivalence) in terms of its fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\), without passing through a choice of a particular object \(F\) of the latter. Now to every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) is associated the functor \(E_X\) on the fundamental groupoid, defined by
\[ E_X(F)=F(X), \]

with values in \(\mathbf{Ens}\). (Such a functor is known in topology under the name of “local system” on the groupoid) \(F(X)=E_X(F)\) may be called the fibre of \(X\) at \(F\), and the functor \(E_X\) the fibre-functor associated with \(X\). The functor \(E_X\) has the following property: for every \(F\), \(E_X(F)\) is a finite set on which the topological group \(\pi_F=\operatorname{Aut}(F)\) operates continuously. For a given covariant functor \(\xi\) of the fundamental groupoid into \(\mathbf{Ens}\), the preceding condition is moreover equivalent to the same condition for one fixed arbitrary \(F\). This being posited:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8

The functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\) is an equivalence of the category \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category of covariant functors of the fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathbf{Ens}\), which satisfy the condition brought to light above.

Indeed, let \(F_0\) be an object of the fundamental groupoid, and let \(\pi_0=\operatorname{Aut}(F_0)\), then the functor \(\xi\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \xi(F_0)\) is an equivalence of the second category considered in 8 with the category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi_0)\), as one observes at once. On the other hand, the composite of the latter and of the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\) is the natural equivalence \(\mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}(\pi_0)\). It follows that the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\) itself is an equivalence.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9

The category \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}\) is canonically equivalent to the category of covariant functors \(\xi\) of the fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\) into the category of topological spaces, satisfying the condition: for every object \(F\) of \(\Gamma\), \(\xi(F)\) is a compact totally disconnected space with topological group \(\pi_F\) of operators.
Here again, one may verify this condition on \(\xi\); it suffices to verify it for one \(F\). The proof is the same as for 8.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 10

Let \((F_s)_{s\in S}\) be a family of objects of the fundamental groupoid \(\Gamma\). Set for \(s, s'\in S\):

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(s, s')=\operatorname{Hom}(F_s, F_{s'}) \]

so that \(S\) itself becomes a connected groupoid and the map \(s\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F_s\) a fully faithful functor of \(S\) into \(\Gamma\), say \(f\). Considering then the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} E_X\circ f\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) into the category of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(S,\mathbf{Ens})\), one obtains a variant of 8 (and 9) with \(\Gamma\) replaced by \(S\). The statement thus obtained reduces to theorem 1 when \(S\) is reduced to a point, and is none other than 8 itself if \(S\) is the set of objects of \(\Gamma\).
We are going to use 9 to define a canonical pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\). For this, we consider the functor of \(\Gamma\) into the category of topological spaces (and even of topological groups):
\[ f\colon F\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{Aut}(F)=\pi_F. \]

This functor satisfies the condition considered in 8, the space with operators \(f(F)\) under \(\pi_F\) is none other than \(\pi_F\), considered as a space with operators under itself by the inner automorphisms. Hence the functor \(f\) corresponds to a pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\) determined up to unique isomorphism, which is even a pro-group of \(\mathscr{C}\) and which is called the fundamental pro-group of \({\mathscr{C}}\), playing the role of a local system of fundamental groups. It is thus a pro-group \(\Pi\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) defined by the condition that one have an isomorphism functorial in \(F\)
\[ F(\Pi)\simeq\pi_F. \]
If \(X\) is an arbitrary pro-object of \(\mathscr{C}\), one has a canonical morphism
\[ \Pi\times X \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \]
which makes \(X\) an object with left operator group \(G\) in \(\operatorname{Pro\text{-}}\mathscr{C}\). It suffices for this to note that for \(F\) variable, one has a canonical map
\[ \Pi(F)\times X(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X(F) \]

\[ \operatorname{Aut}(F)\times E_X(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} E_X(F), \quad \text{or} \quad \pi_F\times F(X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(X) \]

which is functorial in \(F\). It is also functorial in \(X\), hence for every morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) of pro-objects, the corresponding diagram
\[ \xymatrix{{\Pi \times X}\ar[r]\ar[d]&{X}\ar[d]\\{\Pi \times Y}\ar[r]&{Y}} \]
is commutative.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 11

One must take care not to confuse a fundamental pro-object \(P\) (which is not endowed with a group structure, and is connected) with the fundamental pro-group (which is a pro-group, and in general not connected). More precisely, \(G\) is connected if and only if \(\pi_F\) acting on itself by inner automorphisms is transitive, i.e. if \(\pi\) is reduced to the identity element, or again if \(\mathscr{C}\) is equivalent to the category of finite sets. Another essential difference is that \(G\) is determined up to unique isomorphism, while \(P\) is only determined up to non-unique isomorphism.

Let \(E\) be a finite set and consider the constant functor on the groupoid \(\Gamma\), with value \(E\): by virtue of 8 it defines an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), denoted \(E_{\mathscr{C}}\), and which may also be interpreted as the sum of \(E\) copies of the final object \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) of \(\mathscr{C}\). One may regard \(E_{\mathscr{C}}\) as a functor in \(E\), from the category of finite sets to the category \(\mathscr{C}\), and this functor is exact, hence transforms finite groups into \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups, etc. If therefore \(X\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) on which the finite group \(G\) acts on the right, one sees that one may regard \(X\) as an object of \(\mathscr{C}\) having a \(\mathscr{C}\)-group of operators on the right \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\). One will therefore say, by extension of a general terminology relative to objects with \(\mathscr{C}\)-groups of operators, that \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G\) if \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous under \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\), i.e. if the canonical morphism

\[ X\times G_{\mathscr{C}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\times X \]

deduced from the operation of \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\) on \(X\) on the right, is an isomorphism. One says that \(X\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\) if it is so under \(G_{\mathscr{C}}\), i.e. if it is so formally, and if moreover the quotient \(X/G=X/G_{\mathscr{C}}\) is \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\). If one fixes a fundamental functor, whence an equivalence of \(\mathscr{C}\) with a category \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), \(X\) corresponds to a set on which \(\pi\) acts on the left continuously, say \(E=F(X)\). To make \(G\) act on \(X\) on the right then amounts to making \(G\) act on the set \(E\) on the right, in such a way that the operations of \(G\) commute with those of \(\pi\). One then observes at once that \(X\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\) if and only if the set \(E\) is a principal homogeneous space under \(G\), i.e. if and only if \(G\) acts on it in a simply transitive way. (Moreover \(X\) is formally principal homogeneous \(E\) principal homogeneous or empty). Comparing with 3, one sees that if \(X\) is principal homogeneous under \(G\) and connected, then the given homomorphism from \(G\) to the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(X)\) is an isomorphism; and moreover, for an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) to be connected and principal homogeneous under the group opposite to \(\operatorname{Aut}(X)\), it is necessary and sufficient, with the notations of 4, that it be isomorphic to a Galois \(P_i\). In the typical case \(\mathscr{C}=\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), this means that \(X\) is isomorphic to a quotient of \(\pi\) by an invariant subgroup.

Suppose still given a fundamental functor \(F\). Then the datum of an \(X\) principal homogeneous under a finite group \(G\) acting on the right, and of a point \(a\in F(X)\), is equivalent to the datum of a homomorphism from \(\pi\) to the group \(G\). Indeed, to such a homomorphism one associates the set \(E=G\), making \(\pi\) act on it on the left by means of the given homomorphism \(\pi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} G\) and the left translations of \(G\), and making \(G\) act on it on the right by right translation, the marked point \(a\) of \(E\) being the identity element of \(G\). Thanks to what precedes, one thus obtains in an essentially unique way every triple \((X, G, a)\) having the properties envisaged above, since a set with a marked point that is principal homogeneous under a group \(G\) is identified with the latter. In this way, one has a direct geometric interpretation of the functor \(G\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} \operatorname{Hom}(\pi, G)\) from the category of finite groups to \(\mathbf{Ens}\), a functor which is pro-representable by means of \(\pi\), and whose consideration would thus furnish another construction of the group \(\pi\) associated to \(F\).

footnotes.html:
It seems preferable to adopt the more expressive term
“fibre functor”.

pages.title: Exact functors from one Galois category into another
pages.html:
6 Exact functors from one Galois category into another

Proposition 1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C'}\) be two Galois categories, \(H\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C'}\) a covariant functor, \(F'\) a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C'}\) and \(F=F' \circ H\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(H\) is exact, i.e. left exact and right exact.

  2. (ii)

    \(H\) is left exact, transforms finite sums into finite sums, and epimorphisms into epimorphisms (or again: objects \(\not\approx \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) into objects \(\not\approx \emptyset_{\mathscr{C'}}\)).

  3. (iii)

    \(F\) is a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C}\).

The implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is a fact general to categories. Moreover the first form given to (ii) implies the second, as one sees by noting that if \(X\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), then \(X\) is \(\not\approx \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) the morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e_{\mathscr{C}}\) is an epimorphism; one will note that \(F\), being assumed left exact, transforms \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) into \(e_{\mathscr{C'}}\). The second form given to (ii) implies (iii), for \(F\), being left exact hence pro-representable, is amenable to 6 criterion (iii). Finally (iii) implies (i), as follows from the fact that \(F\) is exact, and “conservative” (i.e. satisfies axiom (G 6) of 4).

Let then \(\Gamma\) be the fundamental groupoid of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\Gamma'\) that of \(\mathscr{C'}\). Then if \(H\) is exact, \(F' \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F' \circ H\) is a functor from the groupoid \(\Gamma'\) into the groupoid \(\Gamma\), which will be denoted by \({}^t\!H\):

\[ {}^t\!H (F')(X)=F' (H(X)) \]

which one may also write, with the notation \(F(X)=E_X (F)\) introduced in 6:

\[ E_{H(X)} (F')=E_X ({}^t\!H(F')). \]

This last formula shows, taking account of 8 or 1, that the exact functor \(H\) is determined (up to unique isomorphism) once one knows the corresponding functor \({}^t\!H\). Let us fix an \(F'\), and set \(F={}^t\!H (F')\), then \({}^t\!H\) defines a homomorphism from \(\pi_{F'} =\operatorname{Aut}(F')\) into \(\pi_F =\operatorname{Aut}(F)\):

\[ {}^t\!H\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F \quad (F={}^t\!H (F')=F' \circ H). \]

Moreover the formula above shows (taking account of 8) that this homomorphism has the following property: for every finite set \(E\) on which \(\pi_F\) operates continuously, the group \(\pi_{F'}\) also operates continuously by means of the preceding homomorphism \(\pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\). Applying this to the quotients of \(\pi_F\) by its open invariant subgroups, one sees that the preceding condition also means that the homomorphism under consideration is continuous. Conversely, let us give ourselves an object \(F\) of \(\Gamma\), an object \(F'\) of \(\Gamma'\) and a continuous homomorphism

\[ u\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F , \]

there corresponds to it then a functor from \(\mathscr{C} (\pi)\) into \(\mathscr{C}(\pi')\), manifestly exact, hence by virtue of 1 there corresponds to it a functor \(H\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathscr{C'}\) which is exact, and such that \({}^t\!H\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\) is precisely \(u\). One may also, instead of a group homomorphism, start from a functor

\[ U\colon \Gamma' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma \]

which is such that for every \(F' \in \Gamma'\) (or one \(F' \in \Gamma'\), it comes to the same thing) the corresponding homomorphism \(\pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\) is continuous: such a functor is isomorphic to a functor of the form \({}^t\!H\), where \(H\colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C'}\) is an exact functor determined up to unique isomorphism. Thus:

Corollary 2

For a functor \(H\colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C'}\) of Galois categories to be exact, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist equivalences \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}(\pi')\) which transform the functor \(H\) into the functor \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}(\pi')\) associated with a homomorphism of topological groups \(\pi'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi\).

Corollary 3

Let \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C'}\) be two Galois categories, \(\Gamma\), \(\Gamma'\) their fundamental groupoids. Then the category of exact functors from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathscr{C'}\) is equivalent to the category of functors \(U\colon \Gamma' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\) having the following property: for every \(F'\) in \(\Gamma'\) (or one \(F'\) in \(\Gamma'\), it comes to the same thing), setting \(F=U(F')\), the homomorphism

\[ \pi_{F'} =\operatorname{Aut} (F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F =\operatorname{Aut} (F) \]

defined by \(U\) is continuous.

Consider the fundamental pro-group \(\Pi\) of \(\mathscr{C}\); then an exact functor \(H\) transforms it into a pro-group \(H(\Pi)\) of \(\mathscr{C'}\); we are going to define a homomorphism

\[ \Pi' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H(\Pi) \]

(where \(\Pi'\) is the fundamental pro-group of \(\mathscr{C'}\)), by the condition that for every object \(F'\) of \(\Gamma'\), the corresponding homomorphism

\[ F' (\Pi')=\pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F' (H(\Pi))=\pi_F \qquad \text{(where $F=F'\circ H={}^t\!H(F')$)} \]

be the natural homomorphism

\[ \operatorname{Aut}(F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Aut}(F' \circ H). \]

Since this last is functorial in \(F'\), it indeed defines, by virtue of 8, a homomorphism of pro-objects, and in fact of pro-groups, of \(\mathscr{C'}\). This homomorphism is said to be associated with the functor \(H\).

Let now \(H'\) be a second exact functor, from the Galois category \(\mathscr{C'}\) into a Galois category \(\mathscr{C''}\). It is trivial that one has

\[ {}^t (H' H)={}^t\!H\,{}^t\!H' \]

(N.B. one has here an identity of functors, and not merely a canonical isomorphism). One has an analogous transitivity property for the associated homomorphisms of the fundamental pro-groups.

We are now going to interpret the properties of the exact functor \(H\) in terms of the corresponding homomorphism

\[ u\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_{F} \qquad \text{(where $F' =F'\circ H$)}. \]

It is convenient to introduce the notion of a pointed object of the Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\) (equipped with its fundamental functor \(F\)): it is by definition an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) equipped with an element \(a\) of \(F(X)\). It is thus interpreted as a finite set on which \(\pi_F\) operates continuously on the left, equipped with a point \(a\). Thus the connected pointed objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) are identified, by virtue of 3, with the open subgroups of \(\pi_F\). If \(U\) and \(V\) are two such subgroups, corresponding to connected pointed objects \(X\), \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), then there exists a pointed homomorphism from \(X\) into \(Y\) if and only if \(U\subset V\), and this homomorphism is then unique. Of course, the functor \(H\) transforms pointed objects into pointed objects (since \(F=F' \circ H\)). Let us note on the other hand that a closed subgroup of a group such as \(\pi_F\) is the intersection of the open subgroups that contain it; consequently, if \(M\), \(N\) are two closed subgroups, then \(M\subset N\) if and only if every open subgroup that contains \(N\) also contains \(M\). Thanks to these remarks, one easily proves the results that follow:

Proposition 4

Let \(X\) be a connected pointed object of \(\mathscr{C}\), associated with an open subgroup \(U\) of \(\pi_F\). For \(U\) to contain \(u(\pi_{F'})\) (resp. the closed invariant subgroup generated by \(u(\pi_{F'})\)) it is necessary and sufficient that \(H(X)\) admit a pointed section (resp. be completely decomposed).

By a section — understood: over the final object — of an object \(X\) of a Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\), we shall mean a morphism from the final object \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) into \(X\), which amounts to the datum of an element \(a\) of \(F(X)\) invariant under \(\pi_F\); if \(X\) is pointed, we say that we have a pointed section if it is compatible with the pointed structures on \(X\) and \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\), i.e. if \(a\) is precisely the marked object of \(F(X)\). Such a section is therefore unique, and exists if and only if the marked object of \(F(X)\) is invariant under \(\pi_F\). Finally, an object of a Galois category is said to be completely decomposed if it is isomorphic to a sum of final objects, i.e. if \(\pi_F\) operates trivially on \(F(X)\) — a condition obviously stronger than the existence of a pointed section, when \(X\) is pointed. Proposition 4 follows trivially from the preceding definitions and remarks.

Corollary 5

In order that \(u\) be trivial, it is necessary and sufficient that for every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H(X)\) be completely decomposed.

Proposition 6

Let \(X'\) be a connected pointed object of \({\mathscr{C}}'\), associated with an open subgroup \(U'\) of \(\pi_{F'}\). In order that \(U'\) contain \(\operatorname{Ker} u\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a connected pointed object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and a pointed homomorphism from the pointed connected component \(X'_0\) of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\) (hence that \(X\) be isomorphic as a pointed object to a quotient of the neutral connected component of the inverse image of a pointed object of \(\mathscr{C}\)). If \(u\) is surjective, the preceding condition is also equivalent to the following: \(X\) is isomorphic to some \(H(X)\), where \(X\) is a pointed object of \(\mathscr{C}\).

(By the neutral connected component of a pointed object \(X\) of a Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\), we mean the unique connected pointed subobject of \(X\); it corresponds to the orbit under \(\pi_F\) of the marked point of \(F(X)\), by virtue of 3). Since the fact that \(U'\) contain \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) does not depend on the chosen pointing of \(X'\) (for another pointing amounts to replacing \(U\) by a subgroup conjugate to \(U\)), we see:

Corollary 7

In order that \(U'\) contain \(\operatorname{Ker} u\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) (which may be assumed connected) and a morphism from a connected component of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\). If \(u\) is surjective, this also means that \(X'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\).

Corollary 8

In order that \(u\) be injective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every object \(X'\) of \({\mathscr{C}}'\), there exist an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and a homomorphism from a connected component of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\).

Proposition 9

The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The homomorphism \(u\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\) is surjective.

  2. (ii)

    For every connected object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H(X)\) is connected.

  3. (iii)

    The functor \(H\) is fully faithful.

This last fact means that for two objects \(X\), \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), the natural map

\[ \operatorname{Hom}(X,Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(H(X),H(Y)) \]

is bijective.

Corollary 10

In order that \(u\) be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H\) be an equivalence of categories, or again that the two following conditions be satisfied:

  1. a)

    for every connected object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H(X)\) is connected

  2. b)

    every object of \(\mathscr{C}'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\).

Proposition 11

Let \(H\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}'\) and \(H'\colon\mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}''\) be exact functors between Galois categories, \(F''\) a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C}''\), set \(F'=F''H'\) and \(F=F'H\), and consider the associated homomorphisms

\[ u'\colon\pi_{F''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_{F'}\qquad u\colon\pi_{F'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F. \]

In order that \(\operatorname{Ker} u\subset \operatorname{Im} u'\) i.e. in order that \(uu'\) be the trivial homomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that for every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H'(H(X))\) be completely decomposed. In order that \(\operatorname{Ker} u \supset \operatorname{Im} u' \), it is necessary and sufficient that for every connected pointed object \(X'\) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) such that \(H'(X')\) admits a pointed section, there exist an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and a homomorphism from a connected component of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\).

The first assertion follows from the last statement of 4. The second follows from the conjunction of 4 and 6.

Remark 12

It is not true in general, under the conditions of 8, that \(X'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\). One can show that in order that every connected object (hence every object) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) be isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(u\) be an isomorphism of \(\pi_{F'}\) onto a direct factor subgroup of \(\pi_F\). In practice, however, one directly constructs a homomorphism \(\pi_F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{F'}\) that is a right inverse of \(u\) by means of a suitable exact functor from \(\mathscr{C'}\) into \(\mathscr{C}\).

Proposition 13

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category equipped with a fundamental functor \(F\), \(S\) a connected object of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C}'\) the category of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) over \(S\). Then \(\mathscr{C'}\) is a Galois category, and the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} H(X)=X\times S\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathscr{C}'\) is exact. Let \(a\in F(S)\), and let \(F'\) be the functor from \(\mathscr{C}'\) into the category of finite sets defined by

\[ F'(X')=\text{inverse image of~$a$ under }F(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(S). \]

Then we have an isomorphism \(F\cong F'\circ H\), and the corresponding homomorphism

\[ u:\pi_{F'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F \]

is an isomorphism of \(\pi_{F'}\) onto the open subgroup \(U\) of \(\pi_{F}\) which is the stabilizer of the marked element \(a\) of \(F(X)\).

The proof is left to the reader.

blocks.html: 6 Exact functors from one Galois category into another

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C'}\) be two Galois categories, \(H\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C'}\) a covariant functor, \(F'\) a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C'}\) and \(F=F' \circ H\). Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    \(H\) is exact, i.e. left exact and right exact.

  2. (ii)

    \(H\) is left exact, transforms finite sums into finite sums, and epimorphisms into epimorphisms (or again: objects \(\not\approx \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) into objects \(\not\approx \emptyset_{\mathscr{C'}}\)).

  3. (iii)

    \(F\) is a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C}\).

The implication (i)\(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\)(ii) is a fact general to categories. Moreover the first form given to (ii) implies the second, as one sees by noting that if \(X\) is an object of \(\mathscr{C}\), then \(X\) is \(\not\approx \emptyset_{\mathscr{C}}\) the morphism \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} e_{\mathscr{C}}\) is an epimorphism; one will note that \(F\), being assumed left exact, transforms \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) into \(e_{\mathscr{C'}}\). The second form given to (ii) implies (iii), for \(F\), being left exact hence pro-representable, is amenable to 6 criterion (iii). Finally (iii) implies (i), as follows from the fact that \(F\) is exact, and “conservative” (i.e. satisfies axiom (G 6) of 4).

Let then \(\Gamma\) be the fundamental groupoid of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\Gamma'\) that of \(\mathscr{C'}\). Then if \(H\) is exact, \(F' \mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} F' \circ H\) is a functor from the groupoid \(\Gamma'\) into the groupoid \(\Gamma\), which will be denoted by \({}^t\!H\):
\[ {}^t\!H (F')(X)=F' (H(X)) \]
which one may also write, with the notation \(F(X)=E_X (F)\) introduced in 6:
\[ E_{H(X)} (F')=E_X ({}^t\!H(F')). \]

This last formula shows, taking account of 8 or 1, that the exact functor \(H\) is determined (up to unique isomorphism) once one knows the corresponding functor \({}^t\!H\). Let us fix an \(F'\), and set \(F={}^t\!H (F')\), then \({}^t\!H\) defines a homomorphism from \(\pi_{F'} =\operatorname{Aut}(F')\) into \(\pi_F =\operatorname{Aut}(F)\):
\[ {}^t\!H\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F \quad (F={}^t\!H (F')=F' \circ H). \]

Moreover the formula above shows (taking account of 8) that this homomorphism has the following property: for every finite set \(E\) on which \(\pi_F\) operates continuously, the group \(\pi_{F'}\) also operates continuously by means of the preceding homomorphism \(\pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\). Applying this to the quotients of \(\pi_F\) by its open invariant subgroups, one sees that the preceding condition also means that the homomorphism under consideration is continuous. Conversely, let us give ourselves an object \(F\) of \(\Gamma\), an object \(F'\) of \(\Gamma'\) and a continuous homomorphism
\[ u\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F , \]

there corresponds to it then a functor from \(\mathscr{C} (\pi)\) into \(\mathscr{C}(\pi')\), manifestly exact, hence by virtue of 1 there corresponds to it a functor \(H\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathscr{C'}\) which is exact, and such that \({}^t\!H\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\) is precisely \(u\). One may also, instead of a group homomorphism, start from a functor
\[ U\colon \Gamma' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma \]

which is such that for every \(F' \in \Gamma'\) (or one \(F' \in \Gamma'\), it comes to the same thing) the corresponding homomorphism \(\pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\) is continuous: such a functor is isomorphic to a functor of the form \({}^t\!H\), where \(H\colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C'}\) is an exact functor determined up to unique isomorphism. Thus:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

For a functor \(H\colon \mathscr{C} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C'}\) of Galois categories to be exact, it is necessary and sufficient that there exist equivalences \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}\) and \(\mathscr{C'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}(\pi')\) which transform the functor \(H\) into the functor \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{C}(\pi')\) associated with a homomorphism of topological groups \(\pi'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

Let \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C'}\) be two Galois categories, \(\Gamma\), \(\Gamma'\) their fundamental groupoids. Then the category of exact functors from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathscr{C'}\) is equivalent to the category of functors \(U\colon \Gamma' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma\) having the following property: for every \(F'\) in \(\Gamma'\) (or one \(F'\) in \(\Gamma'\), it comes to the same thing), setting \(F=U(F')\), the homomorphism

\[ \pi_{F'} =\operatorname{Aut} (F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F =\operatorname{Aut} (F) \]

defined by \(U\) is continuous.
Consider the fundamental pro-group \(\Pi\) of \(\mathscr{C}\); then an exact functor \(H\) transforms it into a pro-group \(H(\Pi)\) of \(\mathscr{C'}\); we are going to define a homomorphism
\[ \Pi' \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H(\Pi) \]
(where \(\Pi'\) is the fundamental pro-group of \(\mathscr{C'}\)), by the condition that for every object \(F'\) of \(\Gamma'\), the corresponding homomorphism
\[ F' (\Pi')=\pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F' (H(\Pi))=\pi_F \qquad \text{(where $F=F'\circ H={}^t\!H(F')$)} \]
be the natural homomorphism
\[ \operatorname{Aut}(F')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Aut}(F' \circ H). \]

Since this last is functorial in \(F'\), it indeed defines, by virtue of 8, a homomorphism of pro-objects, and in fact of pro-groups, of \(\mathscr{C'}\). This homomorphism is said to be associated with the functor \(H\).
Let now \(H'\) be a second exact functor, from the Galois category \(\mathscr{C'}\) into a Galois category \(\mathscr{C''}\). It is trivial that one has
\[ {}^t (H' H)={}^t\!H\,{}^t\!H' \]
(N.B. one has here an identity of functors, and not merely a canonical isomorphism). One has an analogous transitivity property for the associated homomorphisms of the fundamental pro-groups.
We are now going to interpret the properties of the exact functor \(H\) in terms of the corresponding homomorphism
\[ u\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_{F} \qquad \text{(where $F' =F'\circ H$)}. \]

It is convenient to introduce the notion of a pointed object of the Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\) (equipped with its fundamental functor \(F\)): it is by definition an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) equipped with an element \(a\) of \(F(X)\). It is thus interpreted as a finite set on which \(\pi_F\) operates continuously on the left, equipped with a point \(a\). Thus the connected pointed objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) are identified, by virtue of 3, with the open subgroups of \(\pi_F\). If \(U\) and \(V\) are two such subgroups, corresponding to connected pointed objects \(X\), \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), then there exists a pointed homomorphism from \(X\) into \(Y\) if and only if \(U\subset V\), and this homomorphism is then unique. Of course, the functor \(H\) transforms pointed objects into pointed objects (since \(F=F' \circ H\)). Let us note on the other hand that a closed subgroup of a group such as \(\pi_F\) is the intersection of the open subgroups that contain it; consequently, if \(M\), \(N\) are two closed subgroups, then \(M\subset N\) if and only if every open subgroup that contains \(N\) also contains \(M\). Thanks to these remarks, one easily proves the results that follow:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4

Let \(X\) be a connected pointed object of \(\mathscr{C}\), associated with an open subgroup \(U\) of \(\pi_F\). For \(U\) to contain \(u(\pi_{F'})\) (resp. the closed invariant subgroup generated by \(u(\pi_{F'})\)) it is necessary and sufficient that \(H(X)\) admit a pointed section (resp. be completely decomposed).

By a section — understood: over the final object — of an object \(X\) of a Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\), we shall mean a morphism from the final object \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\) into \(X\), which amounts to the datum of an element \(a\) of \(F(X)\) invariant under \(\pi_F\); if \(X\) is pointed, we say that we have a pointed section if it is compatible with the pointed structures on \(X\) and \(e_{\mathscr{C}}\), i.e. if \(a\) is precisely the marked object of \(F(X)\). Such a section is therefore unique, and exists if and only if the marked object of \(F(X)\) is invariant under \(\pi_F\). Finally, an object of a Galois category is said to be completely decomposed if it is isomorphic to a sum of final objects, i.e. if \(\pi_F\) operates trivially on \(F(X)\) — a condition obviously stronger than the existence of a pointed section, when \(X\) is pointed. Proposition 4 follows trivially from the preceding definitions and remarks.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5

In order that \(u\) be trivial, it is necessary and sufficient that for every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H(X)\) be completely decomposed.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6

Let \(X'\) be a connected pointed object of \({\mathscr{C}}'\), associated with an open subgroup \(U'\) of \(\pi_{F'}\). In order that \(U'\) contain \(\operatorname{Ker} u\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist a connected pointed object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and a pointed homomorphism from the pointed connected component \(X'_0\) of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\) (hence that \(X\) be isomorphic as a pointed object to a quotient of the neutral connected component of the inverse image of a pointed object of \(\mathscr{C}\)). If \(u\) is surjective, the preceding condition is also equivalent to the following: \(X\) is isomorphic to some \(H(X)\), where \(X\) is a pointed object of \(\mathscr{C}\).

(By the neutral connected component of a pointed object \(X\) of a Galois category \(\mathscr{C}\), we mean the unique connected pointed subobject of \(X\); it corresponds to the orbit under \(\pi_F\) of the marked point of \(F(X)\), by virtue of 3). Since the fact that \(U'\) contain \(\operatorname{Ker} u\) does not depend on the chosen pointing of \(X'\) (for another pointing amounts to replacing \(U\) by a subgroup conjugate to \(U\)), we see:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7

In order that \(U'\) contain \(\operatorname{Ker} u\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) (which may be assumed connected) and a morphism from a connected component of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\). If \(u\) is surjective, this also means that \(X'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8

In order that \(u\) be injective, it is necessary and sufficient that for every object \(X'\) of \({\mathscr{C}}'\), there exist an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and a homomorphism from a connected component of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9

The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The homomorphism \(u\colon \pi_{F'} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F\) is surjective.

  2. (ii)

    For every connected object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H(X)\) is connected.

  3. (iii)

    The functor \(H\) is fully faithful.
This last fact means that for two objects \(X\), \(Y\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), the natural map
\[ \operatorname{Hom}(X,Y) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(H(X),H(Y)) \]
is bijective.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10

In order that \(u\) be an isomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that \(H\) be an equivalence of categories, or again that the two following conditions be satisfied:

  1. a)

    for every connected object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H(X)\) is connected

  2. b)

    every object of \(\mathscr{C}'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 11

Let \(H\colon \mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}'\) and \(H'\colon\mathscr{C}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}''\) be exact functors between Galois categories, \(F''\) a fundamental functor on \(\mathscr{C}''\), set \(F'=F''H'\) and \(F=F'H\), and consider the associated homomorphisms

\[ u'\colon\pi_{F''}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_{F'}\qquad u\colon\pi_{F'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F. \]

In order that \(\operatorname{Ker} u\subset \operatorname{Im} u'\) i.e. in order that \(uu'\) be the trivial homomorphism, it is necessary and sufficient that for every object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(H'(H(X))\) be completely decomposed. In order that \(\operatorname{Ker} u \supset \operatorname{Im} u' \), it is necessary and sufficient that for every connected pointed object \(X'\) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) such that \(H'(X')\) admits a pointed section, there exist an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) and a homomorphism from a connected component of \(H(X)\) into \(X'\).
The first assertion follows from the last statement of 4. The second follows from the conjunction of 4 and 6.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 12

It is not true in general, under the conditions of 8, that \(X'\) is isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\). One can show that in order that every connected object (hence every object) of \(\mathscr{C}'\) be isomorphic to an object of the form \(H(X)\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(u\) be an isomorphism of \(\pi_{F'}\) onto a direct factor subgroup of \(\pi_F\). In practice, however, one directly constructs a homomorphism \(\pi_F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_{F'}\) that is a right inverse of \(u\) by means of a suitable exact functor from \(\mathscr{C'}\) into \(\mathscr{C}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 13

Let \(\mathscr{C}\) be a Galois category equipped with a fundamental functor \(F\), \(S\) a connected object of \(\mathscr{C}\), \(\mathscr{C}'\) the category of objects of \(\mathscr{C}\) over \(S\). Then \(\mathscr{C'}\) is a Galois category, and the functor \(X\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto} H(X)=X\times S\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) into \(\mathscr{C}'\) is exact. Let \(a\in F(S)\), and let \(F'\) be the functor from \(\mathscr{C}'\) into the category of finite sets defined by

\[ F'(X')=\text{inverse image of~$a$ under }F(X')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F(S). \]

Then we have an isomorphism \(F\cong F'\circ H\), and the corresponding homomorphism

\[ u:\pi_{F'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_F \]

is an isomorphism of \(\pi_{F'}\) onto the open subgroup \(U\) of \(\pi_{F}\) which is the stabilizer of the marked element \(a\) of \(F(X)\).
The proof is left to the reader.

pages.title: The case of preschemes
pages.html:
7 The case of preschemes

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian and connected prescheme, and let

\[ a\colon \operatorname{Spec}(\Omega)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

be a geometric point of \(S\), with values in an algebraically closed field \(\Omega\). We shall set

\[ \mathscr{C}=\text{category of the étale coverings of~$S$} \]

and for an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), i.e. an étale covering \(X\) of \(S\), we set

\[ F(X)=\text{set of the geometric points of~$X$ above of~$a$.} \]

Thus, \(F\) becomes a functor on \(\mathscr{C}\) with values in the category of finite sets. The properties (G 1) to (G 6) are satisfied: for (G 1), this is contained in the sorites of I 6, (G 2) follows from 4, (G 3) from 5, (G 4) is trivial by definition, (G 5) follows from 5 and from the beginning of 2, finally (G 6) is proved in 7. One may therefore apply the results of 4, 5, 6. This allows in particular the definition of a pro-object \(P\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) representing \(F\), called the universal covering of \(S\) at the point \(a\), and a topological group \(\pi=\operatorname{Aut}(F)=\operatorname{Aut}^0 P\), called the fundamental group of \(S\) at \(a\), and denoted \(\pi_1(S,a)\). The functor \(F\) then defines an equivalence of the category \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category of finite sets on which \(\pi=\pi_1(S,a)\) operates continuously. This equivalence thus allows one to interpret the current operations of inverse limits and finite direct limits on coverings (products, fibre products, sums, passage to the quotient, etc.) in terms of the analogous operations in \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), i.e. in terms of the evident operations on finite sets on which \(\pi\) operates. Let us note moreover, since the topological connected components of an étale covering are likewise étale coverings, that an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) is connected in \(\mathscr{C}\) if and only if it is topologically connected; by virtue of 3 this therefore means that \(\pi_1\) operates transitively in \(F(X)\). Let us note that in order for an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) to be faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(S\) (as it is already flat and quasi-compact over \(S\)), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be surjective i.e. be an epimorphism in \(\mathscr{C}\), or again that \(X\neq \emptyset\). One then concludes from the criterion 6 (iii) that \(X\) is a principal covering of \(S\) with group \(G\) if and only if it is a principal homogeneous space under \(G\) in the category \(\mathscr{C}\), (as it was defined in 5).

If \(a'\) is another geometric point of \(S\) (corresponding to an algebraically closed field \(\Omega'\), which may be different from \(\Omega\) and which may even have a different characteristic), it defines a fibre functor \(F'=F_{a'}\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) to the category of finite sets, which is again exact, hence isomorphic to \(F=F_a\). Consequently, the fundamental groups \(\pi_1(S;a)\) for variable \(a\) are isomorphic to one another. If one denotes by \(\pi_1(S;a,a')\) the set of isomorphisms (or, what amounts to the same, the set of homomorphisms) \(F_a\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_{a'}\) of the associated fibre functors, one thus obtains a groupoid whose set of objects is the set of the geometric points of \(S\), the fundamental groups being the groups of automorphisms of the objects of the said groupoid. The set \(\pi_1(S;a',a)\) may be called the set of classes of paths from \(a\) to \(a'\). These classes therefore compose in an evident manner. Finally, one may define a pro-group \(\Pi_1^S\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), which one may call the fundamental pro-group of \(S\) or the local system of fundamental groups on \(S\), defined up to unique isomorphism by the condition that one have an isomorphism, functorial in the geometric point \(a\) of \(S\):

\[ F_a(\Pi_1^S)=\pi_1(S;a) \]

(cf. remark 10). In particular, if \(s\) is an ordinary point of \(S\), the fibre of \(G\) at \(s\) is a pro-group over \(\mathit{k}(s)\), inverse limit of finite groups étale over \(\mathit{k}(s)\); one might call this pro-group the fundamental group of \(S\) at the ordinary point \(s\) of \(S\), and denote it \(\pi_1(S,s)\). By definition, these points with values in an algebraically closed extension \(\Omega\) of \(\mathit{k}(s)\) are the elements of \(\pi_1(S;a)\), where \(a\) is the geometric point of \(S\) defined by the said extension. In particular, (taking for \(S\) the spectrum of a field) to every field \(k\) there is canonically and functorially associated a pro-group over \(k\), which one might denote \(\pi_1(k)\), inverse limit of finite groups étale over \(k\), and whose points in an algebraically closed extension \(\Omega\) of \(k\) are identified with the elements of the topological Galois group of \(\overline{k}/k\), where \(\overline{k}\) is the Galois closure of \(k\) in \(\Omega\) (cf. 1). This group \(\pi_1(k)\) does not yet seem to have retained the attention of algebraists.

Let now

\[ f\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

be a morphism from one connected locally Noetherian prescheme into another, let \(a'\) be a geometric point of \(S'\) and let \(a=f(a')\) be its direct image in \(S\). Then the functor “inverse image” induces a functor from the category \(\mathscr{C}(S)\) of the étale coverings of \(S\), into the category \(\mathscr{C}(S')\) of the étale coverings of \(S'\):

\[ f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}:\mathscr{C}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}(S') \]

Moreover one has an isomorphism of functors

\[ F_a\cong F_{a'}\circ f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}, \]

so that \(f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}\) is an exact functor, to which the results of 6 apply. One has in particular a canonical homomorphism

\[ u=\pi_1(f;a'):\pi_1(S',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S;a) \qquad (a'=f(a)) \]

which allows one to reconstitute the inverse image functor, as an operation of restriction of the groups of operators. The properties of the functor \(f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}\) are therefore expressed in a simple manner by the properties of the homomorphism of associated groups, as was made explicit in 6. If in particular \(S'\) is an étale covering of \(S\), then the homomorphism \(u\) is an isomorphism of \(\pi_1(S',a')\) onto the open subgroup of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) which defines the connected pointed étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) (i.e. the stabilizer \(U\) of \(a'\in F_a(S')\) in \(\pi_1(S;A)\)).

If one wishes to interpret the homomorphisms \(\pi_1(f;a')\) for a variable geometric point \(a'\), one must, in accordance with what was said in 6, consider a homomorphism

\[ \Pi_1(f):\Pi_1^{S'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}(\Pi_1^S) \]

of pro-groups over \(S\), and take the corresponding homomorphism for the geometric fibres.

blocks.html: 7 The case of preschemes
Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian and connected prescheme, and let
\[ a\colon \operatorname{Spec}(\Omega)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]
be a geometric point of \(S\), with values in an algebraically closed field \(\Omega\). We shall set
\[ \mathscr{C}=\text{category of the étale coverings of~$S$} \]
and for an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), i.e. an étale covering \(X\) of \(S\), we set
\[ F(X)=\text{set of the geometric points of~$X$ above of~$a$.} \]

Thus, \(F\) becomes a functor on \(\mathscr{C}\) with values in the category of finite sets. The properties (G 1) to (G 6) are satisfied: for (G 1), this is contained in the sorites of I 6, (G 2) follows from 4, (G 3) from 5, (G 4) is trivial by definition, (G 5) follows from 5 and from the beginning of 2, finally (G 6) is proved in 7. One may therefore apply the results of 4, 5, 6. This allows in particular the definition of a pro-object \(P\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) representing \(F\), called the universal covering of \(S\) at the point \(a\), and a topological group \(\pi=\operatorname{Aut}(F)=\operatorname{Aut}^0 P\), called the fundamental group of \(S\) at \(a\), and denoted \(\pi_1(S,a)\). The functor \(F\) then defines an equivalence of the category \(\mathscr{C}\) with the category of finite sets on which \(\pi=\pi_1(S,a)\) operates continuously. This equivalence thus allows one to interpret the current operations of inverse limits and finite direct limits on coverings (products, fibre products, sums, passage to the quotient, etc.) in terms of the analogous operations in \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), i.e. in terms of the evident operations on finite sets on which \(\pi\) operates. Let us note moreover, since the topological connected components of an étale covering are likewise étale coverings, that an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) is connected in \(\mathscr{C}\) if and only if it is topologically connected; by virtue of 3 this therefore means that \(\pi_1\) operates transitively in \(F(X)\). Let us note that in order for an object \(X\) of \(\mathscr{C}\) to be faithfully flat and quasi-compact over \(S\) (as it is already flat and quasi-compact over \(S\)), it is necessary and sufficient that \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S\) be surjective i.e. be an epimorphism in \(\mathscr{C}\), or again that \(X\neq \emptyset\). One then concludes from the criterion 6 (iii) that \(X\) is a principal covering of \(S\) with group \(G\) if and only if it is a principal homogeneous space under \(G\) in the category \(\mathscr{C}\), (as it was defined in 5).

If \(a'\) is another geometric point of \(S\) (corresponding to an algebraically closed field \(\Omega'\), which may be different from \(\Omega\) and which may even have a different characteristic), it defines a fibre functor \(F'=F_{a'}\) from \(\mathscr{C}\) to the category of finite sets, which is again exact, hence isomorphic to \(F=F_a\). Consequently, the fundamental groups \(\pi_1(S;a)\) for variable \(a\) are isomorphic to one another. If one denotes by \(\pi_1(S;a,a')\) the set of isomorphisms (or, what amounts to the same, the set of homomorphisms) \(F_a\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_{a'}\) of the associated fibre functors, one thus obtains a groupoid whose set of objects is the set of the geometric points of \(S\), the fundamental groups being the groups of automorphisms of the objects of the said groupoid. The set \(\pi_1(S;a',a)\) may be called the set of classes of paths from \(a\) to \(a'\). These classes therefore compose in an evident manner. Finally, one may define a pro-group \(\Pi_1^S\) of \(\mathscr{C}\), which one may call the fundamental pro-group of \(S\) or the local system of fundamental groups on \(S\), defined up to unique isomorphism by the condition that one have an isomorphism, functorial in the geometric point \(a\) of \(S\):
\[ F_a(\Pi_1^S)=\pi_1(S;a) \]

(cf. remark 10). In particular, if \(s\) is an ordinary point of \(S\), the fibre of \(G\) at \(s\) is a pro-group over \(\mathit{k}(s)\), inverse limit of finite groups étale over \(\mathit{k}(s)\); one might call this pro-group the fundamental group of \(S\) at the ordinary point \(s\) of \(S\), and denote it \(\pi_1(S,s)\). By definition, these points with values in an algebraically closed extension \(\Omega\) of \(\mathit{k}(s)\) are the elements of \(\pi_1(S;a)\), where \(a\) is the geometric point of \(S\) defined by the said extension. In particular, (taking for \(S\) the spectrum of a field) to every field \(k\) there is canonically and functorially associated a pro-group over \(k\), which one might denote \(\pi_1(k)\), inverse limit of finite groups étale over \(k\), and whose points in an algebraically closed extension \(\Omega\) of \(k\) are identified with the elements of the topological Galois group of \(\overline{k}/k\), where \(\overline{k}\) is the Galois closure of \(k\) in \(\Omega\) (cf. 1). This group \(\pi_1(k)\) does not yet seem to have retained the attention of algebraists.
Let now
\[ f\colon S'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} S \]

be a morphism from one connected locally Noetherian prescheme into another, let \(a'\) be a geometric point of \(S'\) and let \(a=f(a')\) be its direct image in \(S\). Then the functor “inverse image” induces a functor from the category \(\mathscr{C}(S)\) of the étale coverings of \(S\), into the category \(\mathscr{C}(S')\) of the étale coverings of \(S'\):
\[ f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}:\mathscr{C}(S)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathscr{C}(S') \]
Moreover one has an isomorphism of functors
\[ F_a\cong F_{a'}\circ f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}, \]
so that \(f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}\) is an exact functor, to which the results of 6 apply. One has in particular a canonical homomorphism
\[ u=\pi_1(f;a'):\pi_1(S',a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S;a) \qquad (a'=f(a)) \]

which allows one to reconstitute the inverse image functor, as an operation of restriction of the groups of operators. The properties of the functor \(f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}\) are therefore expressed in a simple manner by the properties of the homomorphism of associated groups, as was made explicit in 6. If in particular \(S'\) is an étale covering of \(S\), then the homomorphism \(u\) is an isomorphism of \(\pi_1(S',a')\) onto the open subgroup of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) which defines the connected pointed étale covering \(S'\) of \(S\) (i.e. the stabilizer \(U\) of \(a'\in F_a(S')\) in \(\pi_1(S;A)\)).
If one wishes to interpret the homomorphisms \(\pi_1(f;a')\) for a variable geometric point \(a'\), one must, in accordance with what was said in 6, consider a homomorphism
\[ \Pi_1(f):\Pi_1^{S'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} f^{\scriptscriptstyle\bullet}(\Pi_1^S) \]
of pro-groups over \(S\), and take the corresponding homomorphism for the geometric fibres.

pages.title: Case of a normal base prescheme
pages.html:
8 Case of a normal base prescheme

Proposition 1

Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a field \(k\), and let \(\Omega\) be an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), defining a geometric point \(a\) of \(S\) with values in \(\Omega\). Let \(\overline{k}\) be the separable closure of \(k\) in \(\Omega\). Then there exists a canonical isomorphism of \(\pi_1(S,a)\) onto the topological Galois group of \(\overline{k}/k\).

Let \(k'\) be the algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(\Omega\); it therefore corresponds to a geometric point \(b\) of \(S\), with values in \(k'\). The natural homomorphism of functors \(F_b\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_a\) is evidently an isomorphism, since a \(k\)-homomorphism of a finite separable extension of \(k\) into \(\Omega\) necessarily takes its values in \(\overline{k}\) and a fortiori in \(k'\). On the other hand, the group \(\pi'\) of \(k\)-automorphisms of \(k'/k\) operates in an evident way on \(F_b\), whence a homomorphism \(\pi'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Aut}(F_b)\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Aut}(F_a)=\pi_1(S;a)\). On the other hand, it is well known that the natural homomorphism of \(\pi'\) into the group \(\pi\) of automorphisms of \(\overline{k}/k\) is an isomorphism. One thus obtains a canonical homomorphism \(\pi\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \pi_1(S;a)\), and it remains to show that it is an isomorphism. Indeed, this homomorphism is injective, since an element of the kernel is an automorphism of \(\overline{k}/k\) which induces the identity on every finite separable sub-extension, hence is trivial. This homomorphism is surjective, since if \(X\) is a connected étale covering of \(S\), hence defined by a finite separable extension \(L/k\), then \(\pi\) is transitive on the set of \(k\)-homomorphisms of \(L\) into \(k'\), as is well known.

Proposition 2

Let \(S\) be a connected, locally Noetherian and normal prescheme, \(K=\mathit{k}(s)\) its field of functions \(=\) the residue field at its generic point \(s\), \(\Omega\) an algebraically closed extension of \(K\), defining a geometric point \(a'\) of \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(K)\) and a geometric point \(a\) of \(S\). Then the homomorphism \(\pi_1(S';a')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\pi_1(S;a)\) is surjective. When one identifies the first group with the Galois group of the separable closure \(\overline{K}\) of \(K\) in \(\Omega\) (cf. 1), then the kernel of the preceding homomorphism corresponds by Galois theory to the sub-extension of \(\overline{K}/K\) composed of the finite extensions of \(K\) in \(\Omega\) which are unramified over \(S\).

The first assertion means that the inverse image over \(S'\) of a connected étale covering \(X\) of \(S\) is connected, i.e. that \(X\) is integral; this is none other than (I 1). The kernel of the preceding homomorphism is then interpreted as consisting of the automorphisms of \(\overline{K}/K\) which induce the identity on the sets \(F_a(X)\), where one may suppose the étale covering \(X\) of \(S\) connected. But this means that this automorphism induces the identity on the finite sub-extensions of \(\overline{K}/K\) which are unramified over \(S\), which proves the last assertion.

Remark

Thanks to this interpretation of the fundamental group of the normal prescheme \(S\) in terms of ordinary Galois theory, the definition was known in this case long ago.

pages.title:
The case of non-connected preschemes: multigalois
categories
pages.html:
9 The case of non-connected preschemes: multigalois categories

Let \(S\) be a locally Noetherian prescheme, and let \((S_i)_{i\in I}\) be its connected components. Then the category \(\mathscr{C}(S)\) of étale coverings of \(S\) is equivalent to the product category of the \(\mathscr{C}(S_i)\), which are interpreted in terms of the fundamental groups of the \(S_i\), once a geometric point has been chosen in each \(S_i\). In the application of descent theory for étale morphisms, it is sometimes inconvenient to make a choice, for every \(S_i\), of a geometric point of \(S_i\). It is then more convenient to resort to the natural generalization of 8 in order to interpret \(\mathscr{C}(S)\) as a category of functors on the groupoid of geometric points of \(S\), regarded as the sum of the groupoids corresponding to the connected components of \(S\); the functors in question are the functors with values in the category of finite sets, satisfying the continuity property analogous to the one invoked in 8. In practice, one will have a family \((a_t)_{t\in E}\) of geometric points of \(S\), such that every connected component \(S_i\) of \(S\) contains at least one of them, and one will then be able, as in 10, to replace the groupoid of all the geometric points of \(S\) by the analogous groupoid whose underlying set is \(E\). Of course, these considerations ought to be incorporated into general definitions concerning categories that are equivalent to product categories of categories of the form \(\mathscr{C}(\pi)\), and which may be called multigalois categories. We leave the details to the reader.
